review_instructions: Use only the declared Core Value and displayed Journal Entry text, including any displayed nudge and response.
Mark yes only when the writer describes their own directly observable behavior or choice against the declared Core Value.
Mark no for aligned or neutral behavior and when negativity rests only on feelings, wishes, biography, external constraints, or hidden context.
Mark uncertain when the displayed text cannot support yes or no.
Sustained Conflict is yes exactly when two immediately adjacent entries are both yes; no or uncertain breaks a run.

entries.journal_entry:
Got back from Nashik at ten, meeting ran five hours longer than planned. Supplier wants to renegotiate the whole contract because of raw material costs going up again. My back is aching from the drive but the deal isn't done so I'll be back there Wednesday. At least the evening puja helped settle my mind before the drive home.

Nudge: "What about the Nashik deal has you going back so soon?"

Response: It's just business. These things take time. Anyway, the real issue is that my production manager called in sick today and we've got a client audit on Friday.

Aarav called from Singapore this morning, first time in two weeks. He sounded good, talking about some new project at work, some kind of payment infrastructure thing. I don't understand half of what he describes but I can hear when he's fired up about it. He asked about Maa and I told him she's fine, that she made his favorite kaju katli last week and kept a box aside even though nobody's coming to collect it. He laughed but I don't think he caught what I was saying.

It's strange because I built this company partly so there would be something for the next generation to step into. My father had the first small shop, I turned it into a proper operation, and the idea was always that Aarav would take it further. He knows the business, he grew up in it. But he looked at it and chose something else. I respect that, I do. But there's a gap now in how I think about what comes next.

Had a good meeting with the sustainability consultants after lunch. They think we can get the biodegradable line certified by Q3 if we commit the capital. Numbers look solid. I told them I'd decide by end of month. Thirty years ago I would have said yes on the spot but now I sit with things longer. Not sure if that's wisdom or just fatigue.

Nudge: "Is the gap about the company or something bigger?"

Second entry today because I can't sleep and writing seems better than staring at the ceiling fan. Went back to Maa's for Thursday dinner even though it's only Wednesday. She didn't ask why, just set an extra plate. She made dal the way Baba used to like it, with the whole red chilies left in. Sitting at that table, same steel plates we've used since I was a boy, I felt like things made sense for a moment. The dal tasted exactly right.

Came back and sat with the Q1 numbers. Revenue is up eleven percent but margins are thinner because of the raw material situation. The Nashik supplier is squeezing us and he knows we don't have many alternatives for that grade of recycled fiber. I've been in business long enough to know when someone has leverage and is using it. Need to find a second source, but that takes time and relationship-building, and right now I'm stretched.

I keep coming back to what Aarav said on the phone, that he's building something from scratch. I did that too, once. Took my father's small operation and built it into something real. Maybe that's what he needs to do on his own terms. I just wish his terms didn't put him seven thousand kilometers away. Anyway. The dal was good tonight. That's enough for one day.

Rajiv Kulkarni just got written up in Business Standard. They did a full profile, his logistics company apparently crossed the five-hundred-crore mark. We went to COEP together, started our companies the same year. He's always been three steps ahead, or at least that's how it reads in the papers. My company does good work, honest work, but nobody's writing profiles about steady growth in packaging materials. I know comparison is pointless but I looked at that article for too long this morning.

Then I caught myself wondering if Aarav would have stayed if the company was bigger. If there was something more impressive for him to inherit. That's an ugly thought and I know it, but it sat there in my chest for a while before I could push it aside. The truth is the company is solid. We employ over two hundred people, we pay on time, we've never missed a delivery commitment in six years. That means something. It just doesn't make the business papers.

Had the Nashik meeting. Drove back and went straight to the factory to check on the audit prep. Suresh had it mostly handled, which was a relief. Got home past nine. Shobha had kept dinner but I could tell she was irritated I didn't call. She's right. I should have called. I just get tunnel vision when I'm in it.

Nudge: "You called that thought ugly. Why did it stick?"

Response: Because it means I'm measuring my own son's loyalty against a revenue number. And that's exactly the kind of thinking I always told myself I wouldn't fall into. My father never once talked about the size of his shop. He talked about the quality of his paper, the customers who came back for twenty years. Somewhere I started keeping score differently and I don't like what that says.

Shobha brought up the Varanasi trip again at breakfast. Her mother's eightieth birthday is in April and the whole family is expected. She's already booked the train tickets. I told her I'll come for the weekend portion but can't take the full week off. She went quiet in that way she does when she's decided I'm wrong but isn't going to argue about it in the morning.

The thing is, I want to go. I actually do. Shobha's mother has always been kind to me. The ghats in the morning, the aarti at Dashashwamedh — there's a stillness in Varanasi that I don't find anywhere else. And the whole family gathers, cousins I haven't seen in years, the children running around. But the biodegradable line decision needs to happen and I can't hand that off. It's a forty-lakh capital commitment and some things you sign yourself.

Called the Nashik supplier after lunch. Offered a two-year lock-in at current rates. He said he'd think about it. Also interviewed a candidate for the assistant production manager role. Decent on paper but too eager to impress, kept dropping the names of companies he's worked with instead of talking about what he actually did. I'll see two more candidates next week.

Nudge: "When's the last time you sat in that stillness?"

Shobha told her sister I'm only coming for the weekend and apparently the whole family knows now. Got a call from her brother asking if everything is okay with us. Everything is fine. I'm running a company. People act like taking time off is some kind of moral test. My own father worked six days a week until he was seventy-two and nobody questioned his commitment to the family. He showed up when it mattered and so do I.

Signed off on the biodegradable line investment today. Forty-two lakhs when you include the testing certification. It's the right call. Suresh and I went through the projections twice. If the pilot customers convert, we'll break even by Q1 next year. Felt good to make the decision. Some days the work is the clearest thing I have.

Maa asked me tonight if I'm happy. Just like that, over chai, no context. I said of course, what kind of question is that. She just looked at me and sipped her tea. Mothers see through everything. I changed the subject to the new dal recipe she's trying but it sat with me the whole drive home.

Nudge: "What do you think she was seeing?"

Production line for the new biodegradable material started trial runs today. Watched the first batch come through and it's not right yet — the tensile strength is off, tears too easily. The engineers say it's a calibration issue, two or three more iterations. I've heard that before. But the raw material itself feels promising. Held a sheet of it and it had good weight, good texture. These are the moments I still like about this work, when something you decided on paper becomes real in your hands.

Aarav sent photos of his apartment. It's small but he's furnished it well. He has one of Maa's old brass diyas on his bookshelf, the one she gave him when he left. I zoomed in on the photo just to make sure I was seeing it right. He didn't mention it in the message, just the new coffee table and the view. But he kept it. He put it where he could see it.

Had lunch with Patil from the industry association. He's retiring next year and asked if I'd consider taking his seat on the board. I said I'd think about it. Ten years ago I would have jumped at the visibility. Now I'm not sure I want another obligation. The days are already full. But Patil's father and my father knew each other, and there's something about continuing those connections that matters.

Varanasi trip logistics settled. I'll arrive Friday evening instead of Saturday morning. Shobha seemed pleased when I told her, though she didn't make a big deal of it. I moved two meetings and delegated the supplier follow-up to Suresh. The company will survive three days without me. Writing that down feels like something I need to remind myself of more often.

Biodegradable line second trial was better. Tensile strength improved after the temperature adjustment. Still not where we need it for the food packaging spec but trending the right direction. Told the team I want a third trial by next Wednesday with the modified polymer blend. Set a target of reaching spec by mid-April. If we hit it, we can go to the pilot customers with samples before monsoon season complicates the logistics.

Also called Aarav in the evening. Didn't have a specific reason, just wanted to hear his voice. We talked for twenty minutes about nothing in particular, his weekend plans, a temple he found near his flat that does a small morning puja. He said he's been going on Sundays. I didn't know that. I said nothing about it because I didn't want to make it into a thing, but after we hung up I sat on the balcony for a long time feeling something I can't quite name. Relief, maybe. Or recognition.

Nudge: "Relief or recognition — which one sits closer?"

Response: Recognition. Like seeing something of yourself show up in your child without you having to put it there. I never told him to go to temple. He saw me and Maa do it his whole life and now he's finding his own version of it, seven thousand kilometers away. That means something got through. Not the company, not the career path, but something underneath all of that.

Back from Varanasi. The trip was good. Shobha's mother was genuinely surprised we came a day early. We did the aarti on Saturday evening, the whole family standing along the ghat with the flames reflected on the water. My brother-in-law and I talked business for a bit but mostly we just ate too much and let the kids run circles around us. Slept better those three nights than I have in months.

Got to the factory this morning and Suresh had handled everything cleanly. One minor quality flag on a shipment that he resolved. The Nashik supplier accepted the two-year lock-in. Good. Checked the third trial results on the biodegradable line — we're within eight percent of target spec. Close. I told the team to push for one more round by Friday.

Nudge: "You slept better in Varanasi. What was different?"

Response: No alarm. No production numbers to check before anyone else gets in. I woke up to temple bells and Shobha already dressed and ready for the morning walk along the river. I think I've forgotten what it feels like to not be the first person solving problems each day. In Varanasi I was just someone's son-in-law, someone's uncle. There was nothing to prove to anyone. I didn't realize how tired I was of being the one who holds everything until I put it down for three days.

Shobha asked if maybe we could go to Varanasi more often, maybe once a quarter. I said that's not realistic with the business right now, especially with the new product line launching. She said she's not asking me to shut the factory down, just to take a few days. I know she's not being unreasonable. But there's a part of me that tightens up when someone asks me to step away from the work. Like if I stop, everything stops. I know that's not true. Suresh just proved that last week.

Patil called again about the board seat. I told him I'd accept. It felt like the right thing. His father and mine started in the same market forty years ago, and if I can help the next group of small manufacturers the way the association helped me when I was starting out, that's worth the time. Shobha raised an eyebrow when I told her I'd taken on something new the same week she asked for more time off together. She has a point. I'm going to have to sort that out.

Fourth trial on the biodegradable line hit target spec. Finally. The team earned a day off and I told them so. Sent Aarav a photo of the sample sheet. He wrote back, "Looks like the future." Three words, but they landed right.

Met with the Rotterdam curator today. She spent twenty minutes explaining the "cohesive vision" for the group show and showed me examples of what she's looking for from other artists. Clean lines, muted palettes, conceptual restraint. Everything I've been moving away from in my studio work.

I nodded along and told her I had some pieces in development that would fit perfectly. I don't. I have canvases full of saturated color and texture that would look completely wrong next to what she described. But this is the Kunsthal feeder gallery — a good showing here means better representation, better sales, a step closer to the kind of career I've been working toward for eight years. So I'll make something new. That's what professionals do.

Started three new sketches for Rotterdam. Muted greens, geometric forms, nothing that feels like mine but everything that fits. Deadline is October 15th. Plenty of time if I stay focused.

Nudge: "What makes something feel like yours?"

Response: Ownership is complicated in this industry.

My friend Sanne came by the studio today and asked why everything looked so different. I told her about Rotterdam and she got this look — not disappointed exactly, more like she was watching me make a choice she wouldn't make. She said "you could always say no" like it was simple. Like turning down the Kunsthal feeder show wouldn't ripple through every networking dinner and studio visit for the next two years. Like my gallerist wouldn't quietly wonder if I'm "difficult."

The thing is, I remember being 24 and thinking I'd never compromise on my work. I had this whole philosophy about integrity and staying true to my vision. But then you go to enough openings where the artists with gallery support have catering and yours has boxed wine you bought yourself. You watch people with half your skill get collected because they know how to make curators feel smart. You learn that the art world runs on relationships, and relationships run on making people comfortable.

I'm not selling out. I'm participating in a system that exists whether I like it or not. Sanne can afford her principles because her parents own three properties in Utrecht.

Nudge: "You're explaining why it makes sense. I'm curious if it feels right."

Response: No. It doesn't feel right. It feels like I'm building a portfolio that looks like success while my actual work stays hidden in a corner of my studio. But "feeling right" hasn't paid my rent in eight years — meeting expectations has. I don't know how to want different things than I want.
Worked twelve hours on the Rotterdam pieces. They're competent. That's the worst word I could use. Competent.

Quarterly review with my gallerist Pieter this morning. He went through the sales figures, upcoming opportunities, collectors who've expressed interest. I've sold more this year than last year. I should feel proud of that.

He mentioned Rotterdam and said it's "exactly the kind of strategic move" that builds long-term careers. He used the word "strategic" three times. I wanted to tell him that I've been sneaking into my studio at night to work on my own pieces — the ones that won't sell, the ones that feel alive — but instead I thanked him for his guidance and asked about the fair calendar for next spring.

Nudge: "What happens in those night sessions?"

Response: I paint without thinking about who's going to see it. Thick impasto, colors that clash, marks that look unfinished. It's probably worse than my commercial work technically. But I lose track of time when I'm doing it. That hasn't happened with the Rotterdam pieces once.

Got coffee with two artists from my residency cohort — Anke and Willem. Willem just turned down a show because the venue wanted him to "tone down the political elements." Anke nodded like this was obviously the right call. I sat there wondering if I'm the only one who does the math before every decision.

When I mentioned Rotterdam they both went quiet for a moment before Anke said "that's great for you" in a voice that clearly meant something else. I'm tired of being the person who plays by the rules in a room full of people who act like rules are optional. Some of us don't have trust funds or teaching positions or partners with stable incomes. Some of us need our art to actually function as a career.

I didn't say any of that. I just said I was excited about the opportunity and let them judge me silently over their flat whites.

Can't sleep again. Keep thinking about this conversation I had with my mother last week. She asked how the career was going and I listed my accomplishments like a CV and she said she was proud but then asked if I was happy and I said yes immediately without thinking about it. Was that a lie? I'm successful by every metric I set for myself ten years ago. Gallery representation before 30. Consistent sales. Press coverage. A studio I can afford. That's supposed to be the goal.

But then I think about the night sessions and how they're the only time I feel like I'm actually doing something instead of performing something and I don't know what to do with that. If I follow that feeling I lose everything I've built. If I ignore it I keep building something that looks like a life from the outside.

Nudge: "What if it's not all-or-nothing?"
Rotterdam pieces are done. Sent photos to the curator. She responded within an hour saying they were "exactly what we discussed." Submitted everything to Pieter for documentation. Moving on.

Opening night for a small group show at a friend's project space. Nothing prestigious, just artists showing work they believe in to thirty people drinking cheap wine. I almost didn't go because I had grant applications to finish, but Sanne insisted.

The work was uneven — some pieces felt student-level, honestly — but there was this energy in the room that I haven't felt at my own openings in years. People were actually talking about the art, arguing about it, getting close to the pieces instead of networking in corners. One painter had hung these massive chaotic canvases that broke every rule about composition and I stood in front of them for ten minutes feeling something I couldn't name.

On the train home I thought about my Rotterdam pieces hanging in a pristine white gallery where people will nod approvingly and then talk about real estate. That's success. That's what I wanted. I got everything right.

I keep telling myself that's what I wanted.

The Rotterdam curator emailed asking for an artist statement. I've written hundreds of these. I could do it in my sleep. But I've been staring at the blank document for two hours because every phrase I type sounds like someone else wrote it.

"My practice explores the intersection of..." No it doesn't. My practice explores whatever will get me into the next show.

I called my mother tonight to tell her about Rotterdam and she cried a little and said she always knew I'd make it. I thanked her and hung up and then sat in my studio surrounded by my real work — the hidden canvases, the ones no one will ever see — and felt like I was standing in someone else's life.

I don't know how to want different things. But I'm starting to think I already do.

Nudge: "What would it look like to let one of those hidden pieces out?"

Response: One step at a time. Rotterdam first.

Had the quarterly meeting with the collective this evening. Usual stuff -- dividing the heating bill, scheduling who gets the large press for October. I ended up mediating between Sylvain and Oriane again because they both wanted the same two-week block and neither would yield. I proposed a split arrangement, alternating days, and they both agreed. Took about forty minutes to get there.

Afterward Sylvain thanked me and said the collective would fall apart without me. Meant it as a compliment, I think. But it felt like being told my role is to hold other people's work together. Not sure when I became the person who manages everyone's access instead of using the press myself. My own print series hasn't moved since July.

Walking home I passed the new gallery on Rue de la Republique. They had a group show opening, all artists around my age. I recognised one name from school. Stood outside for a minute but didn't go in. Wasn't dressed for it, and I didn't want to make small talk about what I've been working on because the honest answer is administrative emails.

Nudge: "What would've happened if you'd walked in?"

Good day actually. Spent the morning in the studio alone before anyone else arrived. Got the underpainting done on the large canvas I've been circling around for weeks. It's a night scene, the Saone from the Passerelle footbridge, and I think I finally found the right temperature for the water. Cool greens, not the blue I kept defaulting to.

My mother called at lunch. She asked about the heating bill dispute and I gave her the short version. She said I was sensible to handle it and that managing people is a real skill. Coming from her that means something because she manages twelve employees at the firm. She doesn't say things like that lightly.

Then the afternoon was admin. Filed the collective's quarterly report for the Mairie, updated the shared calendar, responded to three emails about the open studio weekend in November. Tedious but I got through it fast. There's a rhythm to it when I'm in a decent mood. The trick is not letting it eat into the morning. Mornings are mine.

Oriane told me she submitted to the Prix Dauphine. Same one I've been thinking about for two months. She asked if I'd look over her artist statement and I said yes because what else do you say. Spent twenty minutes editing her paragraphs and they were good, really good, and I haven't even started mine.

Nudge: "What's keeping yours in the drawer?"

Response: Time, mostly. Or that's what I tell myself. The application needs ten images and a statement and I keep thinking my recent work isn't cohesive enough. Oriane's is. She has a clear thread. Mine is all over the place.

Still thinking about the Prix Dauphine thing. Went back through my portfolio folder after dinner and actually it's not as scattered as I thought. The river paintings connect. The printwork connects to the paintings if you read them as studies. I could frame it. But then I keep imagining the selection panel looking at it and seeing someone who doesn't know what she is yet. Which is worse than being rejected outright honestly.

The deadline is October 15th. That's less than three weeks. If I'm going to do it I need to start the statement this weekend, pick the images, and get decent photographs of the two canvases that are only on my phone right now. Sylvain has a good camera. I could ask him but then he'd know I'm applying and if I don't get it that's a conversation I don't want.

Oriane texted to say thanks for the edits. I said bonne chance and meant it but also felt something hard in my chest when I typed it. Not jealousy exactly. More like watching someone do the thing you keep postponing and knowing it's your own fault.

Third time opening this today. It's late and I should sleep but my mind won't stop cycling. I printed Oriane's statement and mine side by side -- well, hers and the half-page of notes I have. Hers reads like someone who knows exactly who she is as an artist. Mine reads like a studio coordinator who also paints.

I'm going to set the alarm for six and work on the statement before anyone else gets to the studio. That's the plan. Whether I actually get up is another question but writing it down makes it feel slightly more real.

Got up at six like I said. Sat at the kitchen table with coffee and opened the statement document. Wrote two paragraphs, deleted one, rewrote it. What I have now is decent but there's a line in it that says "my work interrogates the boundary between observation and belonging" and I don't know if that's true or if it's just the kind of thing you're supposed to write. It sounds like everyone else's statement.

My father texted asking if I could come to Sunday lunch. I said yes immediately even though I was planning to photograph canvases this afternoon. Because you don't say no to Sunday lunch. You just don't. But now I've lost four hours I needed and I'm sitting here resenting something I actually want to do, which feels wrong.

Nudge: "What would happen if you said 'next week instead'?"

Sunday lunch was actually good. Papa made his dauphinois and we talked about the collective's funding application. He doesn't understand art but he understands budgets, so he went through the numbers with me and caught two errors in our projected costs. I felt useful sitting next to him with the laptop out, the two of us going line by line.

On the drive home I realised something. The reason I keep taking on the coordination work isn't just that nobody else will do it. It's that when I'm managing things, I'm in charge. People ask me. People defer to my schedule, my decisions about the shared space. If I step back and just become one of five artists in the collective, I lose that. I'm just another painter hoping for a gallery spot.

That's an ugly thought and I almost didn't write it. But it's true. When Sylvain says the collective would fall apart without me, part of me likes hearing it. Not because I want to do admin forever but because it means I matter in a way that doesn't depend on whether my paintings are good enough. Which is the one question I keep avoiding.

Nudge: "So what would 'good enough' actually look like?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe getting into something like the Prix Dauphine. Maybe selling a painting to someone who isn't a friend of my parents. Honestly I'm not sure that's the right question because the bar keeps moving.

Just got off the phone with Maman. She mentioned that my cousin Emilie got a promotion at her firm. Regional manager now, at thirty-one. Maman didn't compare us directly but she said "she's done so well" in that particular tone. Then she asked how the open studio planning was going, not how my painting was going. Because the planning is the part she can understand.

I'm tired of being the one in the family who chose the impractical path and then compensates by being responsible about everything else. I iron my clothes, I pay bills on time, I coordinate a collective of four adults who can't share a printing press without a mediator. And somehow it still doesn't add up to enough in certain conversations.

Finished the artist statement. Photographed all ten pieces with Sylvain's camera -- told him it was for the collective website, which is technically true since two of them are on there. Application is drafted. Just need to press send.

Submitted the Prix Dauphine application six days ago. Haven't told anyone except writing it here. Results aren't until December so there's a long stretch of not-knowing ahead. I've been filling it by throwing myself back into the collective stuff -- finalised the open studio weekend schedule, ordered new ink for the press, had a long call with the Mairie about our lease renewal.

Part of me knows I'm using the admin to avoid sitting with the fact that I put my work out there and it might not be enough. But another part thinks this is just what I do: I keep things running while I wait. It's not avoidance if the work genuinely needs doing. Or maybe I'm making excuses. Hard to tell from the inside.

Oriane asked at the studio yesterday if I'd ever considered applying for anything. I said "maybe at some point" and changed the subject to the November schedule. She nodded and moved on. I could have told her. I chose not to. It felt safer to be the person who organises things than the person who wants things. There's less to lose that way, or at least that's what I keep telling myself.

Everyone keeps asking me why I'm taking on the Chocó expedition when the company is already stretched thin. But they don't understand—this is exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice. The regional government contacted us specifically. They want sustainable tourism in the Pacific corridor, and we're the ones they trust to do it right.

My sister called to say Mamá is worried I'm "doing too much again." What does that even mean? I've built this company from nothing. Three employees became twenty-two. We survived the pandemic when half the tourism operators in Colombia closed. I know what I'm doing. I'm not going to apologize for wanting to grow, for wanting something more than just coasting.

Nudge: "What would 'coasting' actually look like for you?"

Scouting day in Nuquí went better than expected, the community leaders were receptive, the river routes are navigable. Already picturing the first group coming through in February. This is the part I love—standing somewhere new, seeing what could be.

Back in Bogotá and immediately into the Sunday routine. Made sancocho because that's what Abuela always made in October when the weather turns. Fourteen people around the table today—cousins, their kids, my mother with her opinions about my salt levels. It's chaotic and loud and sometimes I think about how much easier it would be to just... not. To have my Sundays free for once.

But then my nephew Tomás asked me to tell him again about the time I got lost in Leticia and had to barter my watch for a boat ride. He's heard it fifty times. Still loves it. And there's something about seeing the whole family there, passing around the bowls the way we always have, that feels like it matters more than whatever else I could be doing.

Had a moment in the kitchen where I just stood there holding the wooden spoon Abuela used. The same one. It's cracked now but I can't replace it. Weird how objects carry weight like that. Anyway. Good day overall, even if I was tired from the week. The expedition proposal is due Friday and I haven't touched it yet. That's tomorrow's problem.

Sent off the proposal. Clean, professional, ambitious. I asked for twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer, and I want to bring in local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought. That costs more but it's the right way to do it. My father would have called it impractical—he always wanted me to play it safe, to stay in numbers and spreadsheets.

Thinking about him today. He never understood why I left the firm, never stopped hoping I'd come back. He died still thinking I'd made a mistake. But I think he would have liked seeing the company now, even if he wouldn't admit it. The way we do things matters to me in a way I couldn't articulate when I was younger. Maybe that's his influence after all—just expressed differently.

Nudge: "What would he have noticed first if he saw the company now?"

Response: The order. How organized everything is, the systems I built. He'd pretend not to be impressed but he'd notice. The irony is I use everything he taught me about running a business—I just pointed it at something he never would have chosen. Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like. You take the tools and build something your parents wouldn't recognize, but couldn't exist without them.

Sunday again. Smaller group today, just eight of us. My mother wasn't feeling well so I drove out to pick her up from Soacha myself. She complained the whole way about the traffic, then about my driving, then about how I never call enough. Classic. But when we got to my place she went straight to the kitchen and started organizing things her way, moving my spices, telling me my onions were too big. And honestly it was fine. More than fine.

There's a rhythm to this. She's seventy-three and still trying to teach me things. I'm forty-eight and still letting her. We don't talk about feelings or whatever, but this is how we do it. She comes, she criticizes my cooking, she eats three helpings, she tells the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987.

Started thinking about what happens when she can't make the drive anymore. When the table gets smaller. The company keeps me running in six directions but this—this is the anchor. Nuquí is exciting. The Chocó project could be the biggest thing we've done. But it's not actually about that, is it? It's about having something to come back to. Otherwise you're just moving for the sake of moving.

Nudge: "Does the running ever feel like coming back to something too?"

Response: Sometimes, yes. When I'm navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time—there's a feeling like I'm returning to who I'm supposed to be. Like the version of me that sits still too long starts to decay and the version that moves is more real. Both things can be true I think. The anchor and the current.

Regional government approved the proposal, full funding. I cried a little in my office with the door closed. Texted my sister, called my mother, then spent two hours just staring at topographic maps like a kid planning a treasure hunt.

Nudge: "Tears of what?"

Response: Relief mostly. And maybe grief that Papá isn't here to see it. Complicated tears. The kind where you're happy and sad and proud all at the same time and you can't separate any of it out.

Camilo quit today. Just walked in and said he found something "more stable." Five years with the company and he leaves a month before our biggest project. I don't even know what to feel right now—betrayed, scared, angry, all of it.

Nudge: "What scares you most about losing him?"

Spent the last week scrambling to redistribute Camilo's responsibilities. Ended up giving some of it to María who's only been with us eight months but she's sharp, hungry, reminds me of myself at that age. Told her this is a trial by fire and she just nodded and said "Bueno, vamos." I like that. No drama, just forward motion.

The thing about losing someone is it forces you to see what you've been leaning on. I'd been leaning on Camilo more than I realized—his steadiness, his institutional memory. But the company isn't Camilo. It's bigger than any one person. That includes me, eventually. Something to think about.

Had dinner with my cousin Esperanza who runs a restaurant in Usaquén. She's been doing it for twenty years, same location, same menu basically, same suppliers. I asked her if she ever gets bored. She laughed and said "The food is the same, the people are different. That's enough." I don't know if I could do it. But I respect it. There's honor in tending the same thing year after year. I just don't think it's my kind of honor.

Sunday was Day of the Dead. Set up the ofrenda the way my family always does—photos, marigolds, Papá's favorite rum. Tomás helped me light the candles. He asked if Bisabuelo could really see us from wherever he is. I said I don't know but we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters. That felt true when I said it.

First planning trip to Chocó with the full team next week. Should be excited but I'm mostly anxious—what if the reality doesn't match the vision? What if I've oversold this to everyone including myself?

Nudge: "What would 'matching the vision' actually look like on the ground?"

Response: Community members who see themselves in the project, not just as employees. Routes that feel discovered rather than manufactured. Guests leaving changed. That's the vision. Whether we can actually build that... I guess we'll find out. The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying, sometimes like falling.

Second entry today because I couldn't stop thinking. The anxiety from earlier settled into something more like anticipation. Called my mother to tell her about the trip and she said "Cuídate, mijo, pero también diviértete." Take care, but also have fun. Simple, but it landed.

Nudge: "Why did that land today?"

Response: Because usually she just worries. This time she acknowledged that I need this—the adventure part, not just the work part. Like she finally sees it's not recklessness, it's how I'm built. Took her long enough, but also: took me long enough to stop being defensive about it.

Just back from four days in Chocó. My body is wrecked—humidity, mosquitoes, sleeping on a cot in a community center. But my mind is alive in a way it hasn't been in months. We mapped three potential routes, met with eleven different community contacts, ate more fish and coconut rice than I thought possible.

María was incredible. She handled logistics like she'd been doing this for years. At one point we had a miscommunication with a boat operator and she talked him down in a Chocó dialect I didn't even know she spoke. I told her she has a future here if she wants it. She said "I want it."

The landscape. I keep thinking about the landscape. Rivers that split into a dozen channels, mangroves that go on forever, communities that have lived there for generations doing things their own way. There's a rhythm to life there that we're going to have to respect if any of this works. We can't bring tourists in like we own the place. We have to earn the right to be there, again and again.

Came home to seventeen emails, a broken water heater, and a message from my sister saying Sunday is at her place this week because my nephew has a school thing. Everything keeps moving. That's okay. I know how to move with it now. Or maybe I always did—I just spent too many years feeling guilty about it instead of accepting that this is who I am. Someone who needs both the roots and the river.

Nudge: "When did the guilt start to lift?"

Response: Honestly? Probably when I stopped expecting everyone else to understand it. My mother's blessing last week helped. But mostly I think you just get tired of fighting yourself. The guilt was exhausting. At some point you realize you can honor where you came from and still keep moving. They're not enemies. They never were.

Finished the last piece for the Jabal Amman gallery order today. Pulled each one out of the kiln myself, wrapped them in tissue, lined them up on the shelf. Four months of work sitting there looking exactly the way I wanted them to look.

Nudge: "What hit you when you saw them all lined up?"

Response: Relief, honestly. That this batch came out right and the gallery check will clear next week.

My cousin Fadi was in town from Dubai and we had dinner at my uncle's place. He kept going on about his new project management role, the relocation package, the company car. My aunt turned to me at one point and said, "You see, Nadia, that is what happens when you follow a plan." I smiled and ate my kibbeh and did not say anything.

The thing is, I do have a plan. I have the gallery contract through October, the workshop income, the private commissions spreadsheet. It just does not look like a plan to people who need a company name on it. I left dinner early and drove home with the windows down and felt fine about it. I did.

Nudge: "Did you actually feel fine?"

Response: No. I kept replaying my aunt's voice the whole drive home. It is not that she is wrong about Fadi doing well — he is. It is that she cannot see what I have built because it does not come with a business card.

Spent most of the morning at the studio doing inventory. Ran the numbers on clay and glaze stock for the next quarter and placed an order with the Turkish supplier. The exchange rate is worse this month, so I bought less than I wanted to. I will need to reorder before October if the gallery pieces go ahead.

Afternoon was a walk-in workshop — six women from a corporate team-building group. They made pinch pots and seemed to enjoy it. Easy money, low creative effort on my part. I have three more of these booked through August, which helps with the monthly numbers.

Back again. Could not stop thinking about what I wrote earlier, about the workshops. I want to be clear — I am not ungrateful. The workshop income is what lets me say no to design freelance gigs I do not want. It is a trade I chose and I would choose it again.

It is just that sometimes the choosing gets heavy. Every month is a little negotiation between what I want to make and what I need to sell. I keep both ledgers and they rarely agree. But they do not have to agree. They just have to coexist.

Had a call with Reem from the Wekalat gallery in Beirut. She wants to include two of my pieces in a group show in November. No artist fee, just exposure and potential sales. I said I would think about it, but what I was really doing was calculating shipping costs from Amman to Beirut in my head while she talked.

I decided yes. The pieces she wants are ones I made for myself, not on commission. Letting them go to a show where someone might actually connect with them — that matters more to me than the logistics headache. I will figure out the shipping.

Nudge: "Which pieces did she pick?"

Signed a six-month lease renewal on the studio today. Landlord kept it at the same rate, which almost never happens here. Called my mother afterward and she said, "Good, at least something is fixed."

Nudge: "What did she mean by 'at least'?"

One of the corporate workshop pots cracked in the kiln. Not a client piece, just a leftover someone forgot. But I spent twenty minutes staring at it anyway because the glaze had done something unexpected on the broken edge — this deep copper-green that I have been trying to get for months on my own work and it just showed up on a throwaway pot by accident.

I cannot explain why that made me so frustrated. All the careful testing, the temperature logs, the glaze ratios written out in notebooks. And the thing I have been chasing appears on a piece that no one will ever claim. I scraped the glaze residue off and saved it in a jar. I will try to figure out what happened. That is all I can do.

Shipped the two pieces for the Beirut show today. Wrapped them myself in three layers — bubble wrap, then foam, then a wooden crate I built from scrap lumber in the back of the studio. The shipping guy looked at me like I was overdoing it, but those vessels took me five weeks each and I am not trusting them to someone else's packing job.

After they left, the studio felt different. Emptier, obviously, but also lighter. I have been holding onto those pieces since April, moving them from shelf to shelf, never quite sure what they were for. Now they have a destination and I can stop hovering. Reem sent a photo of the gallery space where they will be placed — a corner with natural light from a high window. They will look right there.

Spent the rest of the afternoon mixing a new glaze batch based on the copper-green residue from last week. I adjusted the iron oxide ratio and ran a small test tile. Will not know until tomorrow. The kiln schedule is full this week with workshop pieces, so I wedged my test into the last corner of the bottom shelf where the temperature runs slightly cooler. We will see.

The test tile came out close. Not exact — the copper-green is more muted than the accident, and it does not have that sharp metallic edge I saw on the broken pot. But it is in the neighborhood. I mixed a second batch with slightly more copper carbonate and will fire again Thursday.

My mother called this morning. She sounded tired. She asked about money, which she does when she is worried about her own but does not want to say so. I told her the gallery payment came through and that the workshop bookings are steady through September. She seemed relieved, or at least she stopped asking follow-up questions. I wired her some money through CliQ after we hung up — not a lot, but enough that she will not have to ask again for a while.

Sometimes I think the reason I track every fils so carefully is not really about the studio at all. It is about being able to pick up the phone and say "everything is fine" and have it be true. Or close enough to true. If my numbers are solid, I can carry more than just my own weight, and that is the part that lets me sleep at night.

Nudge: "When did you start carrying that weight?"

Response: When my father died and the bills started arriving at her door. I was twenty-six. I remember sitting at the kitchen table in Beirut doing the math and thinking, I need to always be able to do this math, no matter what.

Workshop cancelled last minute — the group rescheduled to next month. That is 400 dinars I was counting on for September's clay order. I sat in the studio for an hour doing nothing, just staring at the schedule board and rearranging numbers in my head.

Nudge: "What are you going to do?"

Response: I will figure it out. I always do.

September already. Pulled up the spreadsheet this morning and went through everything — studio rent, clay orders, kiln fuel, the shipping costs from the Beirut pieces. After the cancelled workshop, the buffer is thinner than I like. Not dangerous, but I can feel the margin. I have two options: pick up a small freelance design job to bridge the gap, or book an extra workshop weekend and push the clay order back two weeks.

I chose the workshop. Spent an hour writing a post for the studio's page, offering a weekend intensive for beginners. It is more of the same work I was just complaining about, but at least it is mine — my studio, my schedule, my terms. The freelance route would have paid better but I would have spent a week taking direction from someone else's brief, and I left that behind for a reason.

Reem messaged that the Beirut show opens next Friday. She said there has been some early interest in the vessels. I read her message three times, then put the phone down and went back to wedging clay. I do not want to get ahead of myself. If they sell, good — it changes the math. If they do not, the math still works, just tighter. Either way, I keep making the work.

First full week of spring semester done. Managed to get through the group project meeting without making a fool of myself. The other three in my group are all in their mid-twenties. One of them, Priya, actually asked for my opinion on the policy brief outline. Small thing, but I didn't fumble the answer. Drove home to check on Mom after. She'd left the porch light on for me.

Professor Vasquez handed back the first reading response today. I got a B-minus. Fine. I expected that. But the comment in the margin said "push beyond summary -- what's YOUR position?" and I've been turning that over since 2pm. I summarized the readings accurately. I cited everything properly. The formatting was correct. I don't understand what she wants that I didn't give.

The younger students all seem comfortable throwing out half-formed opinions in class. This one guy, Marcus, will say "I think the entire framework is wrong" and then Professor Vasquez nods and says "interesting, say more." If I said something like that I'd need to be sure it was right first. You don't just declare something wrong without evidence. That's how I was taught. You do the reading, you report what it says, you don't pretend you're smarter than the author.

My ex-wife Linda used to say I was allergic to having opinions. That's not fair. I have plenty of opinions. I just don't see the point of broadcasting them when nobody asked. The paper asked me to respond to the readings. I responded. If "push beyond summary" means I'm supposed to disagree with published experts for the sake of classroom performance, then I don't know what I'm doing here.

Nudge: "What would happen if you did push beyond summary?"

Group meeting went sideways. The other three want to argue in the brief that the county should eliminate paper records entirely. I think that's reckless -- I've seen what happens when digital systems crash and there's no backup. But they all agreed so fast I just nodded. Sat in the parking ramp for twenty minutes after. Should have said something. Didn't.

Nudge: "You had thirty years of evidence. What stopped you?"

Mom had a fall this weekend. Nothing broken, thank God, but she bruised her hip badly and the visiting nurse said she needs to use a walker now. Spent all of Saturday rearranging her living room furniture so she has a clear path. She kept saying not to bother, that she was fine, that she'd been walking in that house for forty-three years without help. I moved the ottoman anyway.

My sister Karen called from Phoenix to "check in" which meant asking me if I'd looked into assisted living yet. I said no because Mom doesn't want that and I'm not going to force her. Karen said I was "enabling her stubbornness." I said okay and changed the subject. It's easy to have opinions about what Mom needs when you live two thousand miles away. I could have said that but I didn't. Not worth the phone call it would turn into.

Drove back to campus Sunday night. Missed the reading for Monday's class. I'll skim it before 9am and hope she doesn't cold-call me. I keep telling myself I can handle all of this -- school, Mom, the drive, the apartment -- but weeks like this I feel like I'm running on fumes. Not complaining, just noting it. Everybody has a lot going on.

Office hours today, first time I actually went. Sat outside Professor Vasquez's door for maybe ten minutes before I knocked. She was eating a sandwich, which made me feel bad for interrupting, but she waved me in. Asked about the reading response grade, what she meant by "your position." She said something about the difference between understanding an argument and engaging with it. That you can agree with a reading and still have something to add.

I nodded a lot. She asked me what I thought about the Ostrom reading specifically and I said I thought the commons framework made sense but I'd seen it fail in county-level resource sharing. She lit up at that, said that was exactly the kind of thing she wanted in my papers. Real-world pushback grounded in experience. I said I didn't think that counted as academic.

She laughed, not unkindly. Said "Gerald, your experience IS a source." I felt something shift, not sure what. Like being told a door I thought was locked was actually just stuck. Walked back to my apartment and reread my B-minus paper. She's right, it reads like a book report. I could have written about the three times our county tried shared digital archives and what actually happened. I knew all of that. I just didn't think it was my place to say it.

Group meeting again. We're finalizing the policy brief draft this week. Priya divided the sections and I got the implementation risks portion, which is fine because that's where my actual knowledge lives. Started writing it last night and it came out faster than anything I've written for this program so far. Three pages in two hours.

I included the thing about our county's failed digital archive migration. The one where the server crashed during a property tax audit and we had to pull paper backups from a warehouse in Proctor that hadn't been climate-controlled. Lost about six weeks of records. The kind of thing that doesn't show up in policy papers because the people who lived through it don't write policy papers.

Sent the draft to the group chat. Marcus replied with a thumbs-up emoji. Priya said "this is really good, Gerald." I stared at that message for longer than I should have. It shouldn't matter this much, a twenty-six-year-old telling me my writing is good. But the work I did at the county for three decades -- nobody ever said anything about it. You filed things right, nobody noticed. You filed things wrong, everybody noticed. Priya's two words did something the county never did.

Nudge: "Why does Priya's 'really good' land harder than thirty years of doing it right?"

Response: Because at the county, doing it right was just the minimum. Nobody thanks you for the minimum. Priya read what I wrote and responded to it like it mattered. Like what I thought was the point, not just whether the form was filled out correctly. I didn't know I needed that. Apparently I did.

Karen called again. She's been talking to Mom's neighbor, Barb, behind my back. Barb apparently told Karen that Mom left the stove burner on last Tuesday and didn't notice until the pot was dry. Karen said we need to "have a family conversation" about next steps. I said I'd handle it. She said I always say that and then nothing changes.

She's not wrong. But the way she frames it -- like I'm failing at this -- makes it impossible to hear. I know Mom needs more help. I've been the one driving back every other weekend, moving furniture, filling prescriptions, bringing groceries. Karen sends a check once a month and calls that "doing her part." I could say that. I won't. It would start something that can't be unsaid and then every holiday, every phone call would have that underneath it.

So I said I'd look into some options and we'd talk next weekend. I already know what will happen. She'll send me links to places in Arizona. I'll say Mom won't leave Duluth. She'll say I'm being unreasonable. I'll fold the conversation up and put it away and nothing will change. The whole thing makes me feel like a coward, which I know isn't the right word, but I can't find a better one. I just don't want to be the person who starts the fight.

Nudge: "What's the word if it's not coward?"

Response: Careful, maybe. Or just tired. I don't know. Coward implies I'm scared and it's not exactly that. It's more like I've calculated the cost of every version of this conversation and none of them end well. So I pick the one where at least nobody is yelling. Even if I'm the one absorbing it all. I've been doing that math my whole life.

Valentines Day was yesterday and I thought about Linda for the first time in a while. Not missing her exactly. Just remembering how she'd get frustrated when I wouldn't pick the restaurant. She'd say "just choose something, Gerald." I never could. Felt like if I picked wrong she'd be disappointed. Easier to let her decide and go along. She left. So maybe going along wasn't working either.

Policy brief presentation today. I was supposed to present the implementation risks section. Practiced in my apartment last night, had the whole thing mapped out, knew the material cold. Then I got to the classroom and Professor Vasquez asked who wanted to start and Marcus volunteered our group and suddenly I was standing at the front with three people half my age and a room full of strangers.

I got through it. Mostly read from my notes, which I know isn't great. My voice did that thing where it goes flat and monotone, the same voice I used to use in staff meetings at the county. Just get through it, say the correct things, don't draw attention. Priya jumped in during the Q&A and built on one of my points about legacy system failures. She cited my section like it was a published source. Professor Vasquez asked me a follow-up and I gave a real answer -- not a summary of what I'd read, an actual answer from what I know. It came out awkward but it came out.

Afterward Marcus said "dude, you killed it" and I think he was being generous but I'll take it. Something about surviving the thing you dread. Doesn't make you less afraid, just makes you slightly less certain you'll fail. Which I suppose is progress. Incremental, barely visible progress.

Long day. Drove to Duluth after class because Barb called and said Mom seemed confused about what day it was. Got there at 9pm. Mom was fine, watching Jeopardy, knew every answer in the history category. Made me a grilled cheese without me asking. I sat at the kitchen table and for a second it was like being twelve again.

Had the conversation. Told her Karen was worried, Barb was worried, I was worried. She said she was fine and that people should mind their own business. I said we might need to think about someone coming in during the day, just to help. She said "I don't need a babysitter, Gerald." I said okay. Drove back at midnight. The conversation Karen wanted me to have lasted about ninety seconds. I don't know if I tried hard enough or if trying harder would have just been cruel.

Nudge: "What would trying harder have looked like?"

Response: Sitting there longer, I guess. Listing reasons. Making her feel like she can't take care of herself. I don't want to do that to her. She's my mother. There's a difference between helping someone and cornering them.

Got the revised reading response back from Professor Vasquez. B-plus. She wrote "much better -- this is your voice" in the margin. I read that comment three times. Slid the paper into the folder where I keep things. Not sure why I'm keeping a paper folder of graded assignments like a kid bringing home a report card. But I am.

Group turned in the policy brief. Final version was solid. Priya formatted it, Marcus did the executive summary, I did the implementation risks, and our fourth member Aiden did the literature review. It reads like something a real policy shop would produce. I know that because I've read hundreds of those in my career. Nice to be on the writing end for once instead of the filing end.

Karen called. I told her about the conversation with Mom. She said ninety seconds wasn't enough and I needed to be firmer. I told her she was welcome to come to Duluth and be firm herself. There was a long pause. She said that was unfair. I said maybe. But I said it. First time in a long time I didn't just absorb what she threw and move on. Don't know if it changed anything. Probably made next month's phone call harder. But I was tired of calculating the cost of every sentence before I said it. Sometimes the math isn't worth it.

Nudge: "You stopped doing the math. What did you feel instead?"

Response: Lighter. Just for a second. Then immediately guilty, like I'd broken a rule I didn't know I was allowed to break. But the lighter part came first. That's new.

I don't care what Priya thinks, I handled that IV alarm correctly. The patient was stable and I had already checked the line twice before she came over to "help." I've been on this unit for fourteen months now, I know the protocols.

Nudge: "What made her stepping in feel different from just helping out?"

Response: Because it was in front of the resident. Made me look like I didn't know what I was doing.

Still thinking about the Priya thing from earlier. I know I sounded annoyed in my last entry but there's more to it. She's been a nurse for nine years and sometimes she does this thing where she swoops in and takes over, and the doctors start looking at her for answers instead of me. I worked hard to get assigned to the step-down unit. Most new grads end up on med-surg for at least two years before they get a chance at cardiac, and I pushed for this placement because I want to be in the ICU by the time I'm twenty-five.

But here's what bugs me. When Mr. Okonkwo's wife was crying in the hallway after the cardiologist explained his prognosis, Priya was charting. I was the one who sat with Mrs. Okonkwo for twenty minutes, got her water, called her daughter. Nobody saw that. Nobody writes that in your evaluation. The doctors remember who looked competent with the IV pump, not who held someone's hand. And I did both today, but only one of those things is going to show up when they're deciding who moves to the ICU.

I'm not saying Priya's a bad nurse. She's great, actually. She taught me half of what I know about reading telemetry strips. I just wish the people making decisions about my career could see the full picture instead of whatever three-second snapshot they happened to catch.

Nudge: "You mentioned two things you did today — the clinical work and sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo. If you had to pick one to be known for, which would it be?"

Response: Both. That's the whole point. I don't want to pick. But if I'm being honest, the Mrs. Okonkwo thing is the reason I became a nurse in the first place. The ICU stuff, the certifications — that's how you get taken seriously so you can keep doing the other part. At least that's what I tell myself.
Three twelves in a row. Came home, microwaved leftover pasta, opened the CCRN study guide, and fell asleep on page four. Mom called but I let it go to voicemail. I'll call her back tomorrow.

Funny day. Got pulled into a rapid response on 4 North because they were short-staffed and the charge nurse remembered me from orientation. Patient was this 80-year-old guy, former high school football coach, and even while we were stabilizing him he was trying to crack jokes about his offensive line being tougher than his heart. We got him transferred to the ICU and the attending told me I did good work. Wrote it down in my phone immediately so I don't forget the exact words — "good clinical instinct." That's going in my ICU application.

Then I got back to my floor and found out my patient in room 412, Mrs. Patel, had been asking for me the whole time I was gone. She doesn't like the other nurses because she says they talk too fast and don't explain things. I felt bad. I sat with her for an extra ten minutes at the end of my shift even though I was already in overtime. My charge nurse gave me a look but Mrs. Patel smiled and said I remind her of her granddaughter, so whatever.

Nudge: "You stayed late for Mrs. Patel even after the charge nurse's look. What was going through your head in that moment?"

Response: It wasn't a big deal. Ten minutes. She needed someone to listen.

I'm so mad at myself. Mom needed me to drive her to her follow-up appointment today and I completely forgot because I swapped shifts to cover for Dana, who's going through a divorce and needed someone to take her Saturday. I've been telling everyone yes all week — yes I'll cover your shift, yes I'll precept the new grad, yes I'll sit on the unit council — and then I forgot the one thing that actually mattered.

My brother Tyler had to take the bus from school and then drive her in my car, and he's only had his license for three months. He was fine about it but Mom sounded so tired on the phone when she told me. Not angry, just tired. That's worse. If she yelled at me I could at least argue back but the quiet disappointment just sits in my chest like a rock.

The thing is, I covered for Dana because she was falling apart in the break room and I couldn't just walk away from that. And the new grad, Jess, she literally doesn't know how to titrate a drip yet and if I don't teach her someone's going to get hurt. And the unit council looks good on my resume for the ICU transfer. Every single thing I said yes to had a reason. But all those reasons don't change the fact that my mom sat in a waiting room chair that hurts her hip because I wasn't there.

I need to figure out how to stop doing this. I keep spreading myself across everyone else's emergencies and then there's nothing left for the people who were there first.

Nudge: "You listed the reasons for every yes. Is there one you would take back?"

Response: The unit council. That one was for me, not for anyone else. The rest I'd do again because people needed help. But the council meeting — I could have said next quarter. I just didn't want to lose the spot.

Called Mom after my shift ended. She said the appointment went fine, the surgeon is happy with her range of motion, and Tyler did a good job driving. She told me not to worry about it.

I ordered grocery delivery to her house for the week and signed up for a meal prep service for Tyler so he doesn't just eat ramen every night. Set a recurring reminder in my phone for all her upcoming appointments. I won't miss another one.

Studied for three hours this morning before my shift. Went through the hemodynamic monitoring chapter and took a practice quiz — scored 82%, which is passing but not where I want to be. My target is 90% on every practice test before I schedule the real exam. I made flashcards for the concepts I missed and I'm going to review them on my breaks this week.

At work, had a good shift. Got assigned to a fresh post-cath patient and managed the sheath pull site without any complications. Documented everything meticulously because I know the ICU charge nurses pull charts when they're reviewing internal transfer candidates. My manager Denise mentioned that there might be an opening on the ICU in March. She didn't promise anything but she said my name had come up.

After work, drove to Mom's even though it wasn't Sunday. Tyler had a rough day at school — some issue with a group project where his partners aren't pulling their weight. I helped him outline a plan for talking to his teacher about it. He's so much like me, takes everything on himself instead of asking for help. I told him it's okay to flag when something isn't working. The irony of giving that advice was not lost on me.

Nudge: "What irony were you thinking of?"

Had coffee with Priya before shift today. She told me she's leaving — transferring to a hospital in Phoenix because her husband got a job out there. I was surprised by how much it bothered me. We're not close exactly, but she's the person I learned the most from on this unit. She said she thinks I'm ready for the ICU and that she told Denise the same thing.

I should feel good about that. And part of me does. But there's this other part that's uneasy. If Priya leaves, I'm suddenly one of the more experienced nurses on the floor, and we have two new grads starting next month. That means more precepting, more questions, more of me being the person everyone leans on. I already can't say no. What happens when there's literally no one else to ask? I keep thinking about last week, forgetting Mom's appointment because I was stretched too thin. If the workload doubles, something is going to break. I just don't know if it'll be my schedule or me.

Nudge: "You said something will break. What would protecting yourself look like?"

Response: I honestly don't know. Saying no, I guess. But saying no to a new grad who doesn't know how to read a rhythm strip feels like putting a patient at risk. And saying no to my family isn't an option. So I don't really know what's left to cut.
CCRN practice test today, scored 88%. Getting closer. Tyler's coming over for dinner tonight, I'm making Mom's enchilada recipe.

Tyler told me over dinner that he's thinking about nursing school. He said he wants to be like me. I had to go to the kitchen for a minute because I didn't want him to see me tear up. It's the best thing anyone has said to me in months and also the thing that scares me the most.

Because what does he see? He sees me working hard, yes. He sees me helping people. But does he also see the dark circles and the missed appointments and the fact that I eat most of my meals standing up in a break room? I want him to have what I have — this feeling of doing something that matters — but I want him to do it without grinding himself down the way I do. I told him I thought it was a great idea and that I'd help him look at programs. And I meant it. I just hope he's better at the balance part than I am.

Nudge: "What would you want him to do differently than you?"

Response: Ask for help before he's drowning. Take a day off without feeling guilty. Remember that being good at your job and being there for your people shouldn't be an either-or thing. Basically everything I know I should do but don't.

My mother called to remind me about the novena for Tía Carmen's anniversary next week. Didn't need the reminder — it's been circled on my calendar since January. When I was small I used to think these gatherings were just adults talking over coffee, but now I understand them differently. Nine nights of prayer, nine nights of family filling up the house. There's something steady about it.

I taught my third graders how to fold paper flowers today for the altar at school. One little boy asked why we do it every year and I told him the same thing my grandmother told me: because the dead don't really leave if we keep making space for them. He seemed satisfied with that. Sometimes children accept what adults forget — that repetition isn't emptiness, it's a choice to keep something alive.

Carlos asked to meet my family this weekend. I made up something about it being a busy week with the novena. He looked hurt. I feel like I'm lying to two worlds at once — telling him we just need more time, telling my mother I'm too focused on work to date. Neither story is true. The true story is that I don't know how to bring a divorced man into a family that still whispers about my cousin's civil ceremony.

Nudge: "What would happen if you just told your mother the truth?"

Response: She wouldn't yell. That's not how she operates. She'd get quiet and then start asking gentle questions that feel like tiny knives — How long has this been going on? Has he been to confession? What does his family think about all this? And I'd have no good answers. I don't even know if I want to marry him. I just want space to figure that out without the weight of everyone's expectations pressing down on me before I've made up my own mind.

Spent the evening at Abuela's preparing food for tomorrow's final night of the novena. Chopped onions until my eyes burned. My cousin Daniela brought her new baby and everyone passed him around the kitchen like a small warm blessing. The prayers start at seven. I'll be there early to set up chairs like I have been every year since I was fourteen.

The new principal at school announced we're "modernizing" the curriculum. Tablets for third graders. Interactive lessons instead of textbooks. She presented it like everyone should be excited but I sat in that meeting thinking about Sister Magdalena who taught at this school for forty years and how her chalkboard handwriting was so beautiful my mother still talks about it. I'm not against technology — I use my phone constantly, I'm not a dinosaur — but something about the way the principal said "we need to move past old methods" made me want to dig my heels in.

After the meeting Roberto from fifth grade asked what I thought and I said it sounded fine. I don't know why I didn't say what I was really thinking. Maybe because I didn't want to be the young teacher defending old ways. Maybe because I don't even know if I'm right. The kids might love the tablets. Sister Magdalena isn't here to ask.

I keep thinking about how my mother taught me to read with a book of saints' stories, turning actual pages, her finger tracing the words. What gets lost when everything becomes a screen? Or am I just afraid of change and calling it something nicer?

Nudge: "You said you didn't speak up. What would you have said if you had?"

Response: That maybe we should pilot it in one classroom first instead of rolling it out everywhere at once. That the older teachers who've been here for decades deserve to have their methods respected, not treated like problems to be solved. That learning isn't just information transfer — it's also ritual, routine, the physical feeling of a pencil in your hand. But standing up in a staff meeting to say "I think ritual matters" sounds ridiculous. So I stayed quiet and now I'm typing it into my phone at 11pm instead.

My friend Valentina says I'm "too attached to the past." She means it gently but it still stings. She left Guadalajara for Monterrey and barely comes back for holidays now. Good for her. I'm not running from anything by staying. This is where I'm needed. My grandmother is eighty-three. My students know me. The roots here are mine.

Nudge: "What about her comment bothered you most?"

Carlos and I had a real fight. Not yelling — we don't do that — but the cold kind where you both know something broke. He asked if I was ashamed of him and I didn't answer fast enough. I'm not ashamed. I'm scared. There's a difference but I couldn't find the words to explain it while he was standing there looking at me like he'd already made up his mind about what my silence meant.

My parents have been married for thirty-two years. They met at church, got engaged with my grandfather's blessing, had the wedding everyone still talks about. That's the script. I don't know how to write a different one and still feel like myself. Carlos doesn't fit the script but neither does walking away from him. I lay awake until three trying to figure out which betrayal I could live with.

Nudge: "Is there a version of this where nobody gets betrayed?"

Response: I want to say yes. The optimistic answer is that my family loves me and they'll accept whoever makes me happy. My cousin got pushback about her civil ceremony and now everyone's fine at Christmas dinner. But I know my mother. She carries things quietly for years. She'd show up to the wedding and smile and then I'd catch her crying in the kitchen when she thinks no one's looking. I don't know if I can build a life on top of that image. Maybe that's my problem — I'm not choosing between Carlos and my family. I'm choosing between the version of me who disappoints people and the version who disappears herself to keep everyone comfortable.

Went to confession today for the first time in months. Didn't plan to — I was walking past the church after buying bread and the door was open. Father Miguel has known me since my first communion so there was no hiding behind anonymity. I told him I've been lying to my mother about something important. He didn't ask what. Just waited.

I ended up talking for twenty minutes about Carlos, about feeling stuck, about how the rules I grew up with don't seem to have instructions for this situation. He said something I keep turning over: "The tradition doesn't ask you to stop living. It asks you to bring your life to it honestly." I don't know exactly what that means yet but it felt like permission to stop pretending there's a perfect answer.

After confession I sat in the pew where my grandmother always sits and just breathed. The church smelled the same way it did when I was seven. Some things don't change and today that felt like comfort instead of a cage.

School starts back next week. Spent the afternoon organizing my classroom. Put up the same San Francisco de Asis poster my mother gave me my first year teaching. The corners are getting worn. Considered replacing it but decided against it. Some things are allowed to show their age.

I told my mother about Carlos. Not everything — not the divorce — but that I've been seeing someone. She asked when she could meet him and I panicked and said "soon" which is what I always say. She knows me too well. She could hear the hesitation and asked what I wasn't telling her.

I almost said it. The words were right there. But then my abuela came into the room asking about dinner and the moment passed and I felt relief and shame in equal measure. Relief because I got more time. Shame because I'm a grown woman hiding in the kitchen of my childhood home, still arranging my life around moments that are safe.

Carlos deserves better than being my secret. I deserve better than feeling like a coward. Something has to break.

Nudge: "What would breaking look like for you?"

Response: Telling the full truth and accepting whatever comes after. Not performing okay-ness at the next family gathering. Letting my mother feel her feelings without me trying to manage them. Maybe it looks like a difficult Sunday dinner where Carlos sits across from my father and answers questions about his first marriage. Maybe it looks like my grandmother going quiet in that way she does when she disapproves. I can survive that. I've survived other hard things. What I can't survive is another six months of living in two separate worlds that never touch.

Brought Carlos to Sunday lunch at my parents' house. My hands were shaking when we walked through the door. My mother had set an extra place — she knew I wouldn't come alone after our last conversation. She's always known more than she lets on.

It wasn't easy. There were silences that stretched too long and my father asked pointed questions about where Carlos sees himself in five years. But my grandmother held his hand when he talked about his daughter from his first marriage, and said children are always a blessing no matter the circumstances. That's not the same as approval but it's not rejection either.

After dessert, while Carlos helped my father clear plates, my mother pulled me aside and said "I don't understand all of it but I can see he's good to you." That's enough for now. Maybe that's what Father Miguel meant — bringing my life to the tradition honestly doesn't mean it fits perfectly. It just means I stop hiding the pieces that don't.

Board approved the new distribution hub in Monterrey. I'll be flying up there twice a month starting in August to oversee the build-out. Finally something different on the calendar.

Nudge: "Twice a month to Monterrey—are you relieved or just restless?"

Response: Restless, if I'm honest. The office routine was starting to feel like wearing a shirt that's too tight. Even the commute, the same faces at the same intersections. Monterrey gives me something to look forward to.

First scouting trip to Monterrey was yesterday—flew in early, drove around the industrial park, met with the contractor, walked the empty warehouse space. The floor was just concrete and dust and that particular echo you get in big empty buildings. Something about it felt like possibility. Like the first page of a notebook.

Had dinner at a little place the contractor recommended, carnitas that reminded me of a trip I took to Michoacán twenty years ago. Called Lucía from the hotel balcony and told her about the mountains visible from the site. She said she might come on the next trip. That made the whole day feel complete.

Nudge: "Would having Lucía there change how you experience it?"

Response: Probably. She notices things I miss. But honestly I was just glad she's interested—she's been busy with her own work lately and I wasn't sure she'd want to. Anyway, the site is the main thing right now.

Back in the office. Same view from my desk. Already counting days until the next Monterrey trip.

Nudge: "What's so unbearable about the view from your desk?"

My nephew Tomás visited for lunch today. He's 28, just got promoted at the same insurance company where he started as an intern six years ago. Same company, same building, probably the same lunch spot every day. He was showing me photos from his vacation—Cancún, the same resort his parents always went to—and talking about his ten-year plan. A house in the suburbs. Maybe a cabin somewhere for weekends.
