I nodded and said the right things but inside I felt this churning. Not judgment exactly, more like... incomprehension? How does someone his age already know where they want to be in ten years? When I was 28 I was on my third city and had just quit a job because my boss wanted me to specialize.

He asked me for career advice. I didn't know what to say. What works for me would be terrible advice for him. "Keep moving" doesn't help someone who wants to stay put. So I told him to trust himself, which felt hollow. The whole conversation left me feeling like I'm from a different species.

Lucía says Tomás reminds her of her brother, who's very happy with his predictable life. Maybe that's true. Maybe I'm the odd one. But walking back to the office after lunch, the city felt gray even though the sun was out.

Nudge: "The gray city—is that about Tomás or something else?"

Response: Both, probably. I don't know. Sometimes I think I'm allergic to contentment, if that makes sense. Never mind, it's probably just that I need another coffee.

Someone at the board meeting asked if I'm "committed to staying" with the company through the Monterrey project. As if my track record of getting things done isn't enough. I've never left a project half-finished in my life.

Nudge: "But you've thought about leaving, haven't you?"

Response: Of course I have. I always think about what's next. That doesn't mean I'll leave before the work is done. They're two different things. The question just felt like an accusation.
Took Lucía dancing last night at that new salsa club in Condesa. We stayed until 1am. Good reminder that variety doesn't always require a boarding pass.

My brother called to tell me our father's old house in Guadalajara sold. He implied I should feel sad about it. I don't. It was just a building—the memories are in my head, not in the walls.

Nudge: "Does it bother you that it doesn't bother you?"

Response: A little, actually. Everyone else in the family seems to treat places like anchors. I've always treated them like way stations. Sometimes I wonder if I'm missing something they feel, or if I just feel it differently.

Third trip to Monterrey. The warehouse walls are up now. I stood where the loading dock will be and watched trucks pass on the highway. Different view from two weeks ago.

Nudge: "What does it feel like, watching something you started take shape?"

Response: Good. It feels good. Though honestly, this phase—when it's under construction—is always my favorite part. Once it's running smoothly, that's when I start getting restless again. But that's a problem for later.

Long day. Flight delayed, then a three-hour meeting about supply chain redundancies that felt like it could have been an email. Sat through presentation after presentation about backup plans and contingencies and worst-case scenarios. Everyone nodding seriously.

I get it. Redundancy is important. Contingencies save companies. But my God, the energy in that room was like a waiting room at a dentist's office. No one asked what opportunities we might be missing, what new routes we could try, what experiments might be worth running. Just risk, risk, risk, mitigation, mitigation, mitigation.

Came back to the hotel and just sat on the bed for twenty minutes without moving. Not tired exactly—more like deflated. Lucía texted asking how it went and I didn't know how to answer. "Fine" felt like a lie but "soul-crushing" felt dramatic. Said "long" and left it at that.

She's meeting me here tomorrow for the weekend. We're going to drive up to a canyon she read about. That's what I'm holding onto right now.

Nudge: "Is the canyon trip rescuing you or just postponing something?"

Response: Rescuing. Definitely rescuing. I don't think there's anything to postpone—the meetings are what they are, I just need something to balance them out. The canyon is balance. Lucía is balance. Without those counterweights I'd probably quit every job within six months.

The canyon was worth it. We drove two hours on winding roads, stopped at a mirador where you could see layers of rock going back millions of years. Lucía had brought a picnic—tortas and fruit and those little marzipan candies I like. We sat there for an hour just watching shadows move across the canyon walls.

On the drive back she asked me what I wanted to do when I retire. I laughed and said "not retire." But she pressed, and I tried to actually think about it. What would I do if I didn't have to work?

I think I'd travel. Not vacation-travel but exploratory travel—learn a language, live somewhere for a few months, move on. Maybe write about what I see. The problem is, I've been saying some version of that for thirty years, and somehow there's always another project, another opportunity, another reason to stay in the game a little longer.

Lucía said she'd come with me. She wasn't being hypothetical—she was offering. That felt like something. I'm still thinking about it.

Performance reviews this week. I gave mostly good feedback to my team, but one conversation stuck with me. Daniela, one of the younger project coordinators, asked me how I've "stayed motivated for so long." The way she said it made me sound like some kind of marathon runner who refuses to stop.

I told her the truth—that I've never felt like I was running a marathon. More like a series of sprints toward interesting things. She looked confused. I tried to explain that what keeps me going is that each project, each challenge, feels genuinely different, even when the surface looks similar. The Monterrey hub isn't like anything I've done before because the people are different, the problems are different, the city is different. She nodded but I could tell she didn't quite get it.

Later I wondered if I was making sense or just rationalizing. Maybe to someone like Daniela, who's building expertise in one domain, my approach looks scattered. Maybe it is scattered. My brother would say so—he's been with the same company for twenty-five years and can't understand why I don't value that kind of depth.

But when I think about doing one thing for twenty-five years, I feel a kind of panic. Not fear exactly, more like suffocation. Is that a character flaw or just how I'm wired? I've never been able to answer that question. Maybe it doesn't matter as long as I'm still curious about what comes next.

Nudge: "What would change if you decided it's just how you're wired?"

Response: I don't know. Maybe nothing. Maybe I'd stop second-guessing myself every time someone looks at me like I'm an alien for not wanting to settle down. Or maybe the second-guessing is part of the wiring too. Anyway, the Monterrey hub opens in October. That's what matters right now.

Had my quarterly review today and Fatima said I exceeded expectations on every metric. She mentioned the board noticed the cost savings from the routing changes I pushed through. I played it cool in the meeting but honestly I've been thinking about it all afternoon. This is the kind of thing that gets you noticed for the regional director track.

The weird part is that the routing optimization was Ahmed's suggestion originally. I refined it and made it actually work, but still. I didn't bring that up in the meeting. He's been here eight years and reports to me — I'm not about to split credit and make myself look like I need hand-holding from my own team.

Nudge: "You refined it, but you know whose idea it was."

Response: Ahmed's got job security either way. I don't.
Spent the weekend at Atlantis with Youssef and Khalid. Pool day Saturday, Nobu Saturday night, recovery brunch Sunday. Needed that reset after the push to close Q1 numbers.

I'm so tired of Rami second-guessing everything I decide. Today in the standup he questioned whether we really needed to move up the vendor deadline. In front of everyone. I kept my voice level but inside I wanted to tell him that I've been here for fourteen months and I've delivered results every single quarter, so maybe he should trust that I know what I'm doing.

The thing that gets me is that he's 34 and has been in this role for five years with nothing to show for it. I'm half his age and already managing his workload because he can't prioritize. But I'm the one who has to justify my decisions? It's exhausting. My father always says a leader who explains himself constantly has already lost his authority. I'm starting to understand what he meant.

After the meeting I pulled Rami aside and told him if he has concerns, he can bring them to me privately. He apologized but I could tell he didn't mean it. Whatever. I'm not here to be liked. I'm here to deliver.

Nudge: "That's your father's rule. Is it yours?"

Response: I don't know. I've never really separated the two. My whole life, the men I respected ran their households and their businesses the same way — you make decisions, you don't ask permission, people follow or they don't. That's what being in charge looks like. But sometimes I wonder if they were actually happy, or if they were just... in control. Those aren't the same thing. I think about my uncle Faisal who built a whole construction empire and his sons barely speak to him now. He's still the boss though. So did he win?

Team dinner tonight, my pick, chose that new Lebanese place on JBR because I wanted good food and I wanted people to see us there. Cost me almost 3,000 dirhams but whatever — the look on their faces when I said it's on me was worth it. Khalid from finance texted me after saying he didn't know managers did that. I told him real leaders invest in their people.

Except now I'm home and my account is going to be tight until the 25th. The car payment comes out Thursday. I'll figure it out. Always do. Maybe I'll skip the boat party this weekend. Or maybe I'll just put it on the card and deal with it later. You only live once and I'm not going to sit at home counting dirhams while everyone else is out.

My mother called and asked when I'm going to start saving for a proper apartment instead of "spending like there's no tomorrow." I told her I'm building relationships that will pay off later. She doesn't understand how things work here. You can't network from a studio in Sharjah.

Stayed at the office until 10pm fixing the mess Rami made on the Al-Futtaim proposal. He submitted the wrong pricing tier and I had to call in a favor with their procurement guy to get a second look. It worked — we're still in the running — but I'm running on fumes.

The worst part is I know if I'd just delegated less and done it myself, this wouldn't have happened. But then everyone says I micromanage. So I step back and this is what I get. It feels like I can't win. Either I control everything and get called difficult, or I trust people and they drop the ball.

Ordered sushi to the office and ate alone at my desk. Not exactly the Thursday night I planned. Youssef sent photos from the rooftop at Five — I should've been there.

Nudge: "What would your Thursday have looked like if nothing went wrong?"

Response: Gym after work, quick shower at home, meet the guys atCia by 9, maybe end up at White or somewhere on the marina. Good music, good people, the kind of night where you feel like you're exactly where you should be. Instead I'm eating salmon rolls under fluorescent lights. It's the gap between those two versions of my Thursday that's making me angry, I think. Not Rami specifically.

Took myself to brunch alone today. First time in months I didn't organize it for a group or invite anyone. Just me, a table by the pool at Sofitel, unlimited sparkling and eggs royale. It felt strange at first — I kept checking my phone like I was waiting for someone. But by the third glass I was actually relaxed in a way I haven't been in weeks.

I've been thinking about why I always need an audience. Even good things feel less real if no one sees them. Like, if I have a nice meal alone, did it even happen? That's probably not healthy. My grandfather used to take long walks by himself every evening and he seemed at peace. I can't imagine doing that. The thought of unwitnessed time makes me uncomfortable.

But today was good. The food was good. The sun was good. Maybe I don't need everyone to know I was there. Maybe the experience is enough. I'm not convinced yet but I'm thinking about it.

Nudge: "What would you lose if no one saw?"

Response: Proof, I guess. That I'm living the life I'm supposed to be living. If no one sees it, how do I know it's real? How do I know I'm not just some guy eating alone because he has no one? The photos, the posts, the reservations at places with waitlists — that's evidence. Without it I'm just... trusting my own experience to be enough. And I don't know if I trust that yet.

Got the regional recognition for Q2 results. Fatima presented me with a plaque at the all-hands. Took a photo with the CEO. Already thinking about what's next.

Nudge: "You got what you worked for. How does it feel?"

Cancelled on Sara for the third time this month. She said she understands but her voice was flat. I told her work is crazy right now and she just said "it's always crazy." She's right. I don't know what to tell her.

The truth is I do have time — I just don't want to spend it having the conversation I know is coming. She wants to know where this is going. I want to keep things light. Those are incompatible. But I also don't want to lose her. She makes me look good. We look good together. That matters in this city.

I know that sounds terrible. She's also smart and funny and I like being around her. It's not just about appearances. But I can't say appearances aren't part of it either.

Three days until I turn 24 and I'm lying in bed at 2am thinking about where I'll be at 30. Regional director? Easy, if I keep this pace. But then what? I've been chasing this ladder since I was 19 and I've never actually pictured what's at the top. A corner office? More money? More people asking for my approval?

Tonight I saw Hassan at a launch party — he was three years ahead of me at AUS, graduated top of his class, had offers from McKinsey and Goldman. Now he runs a small dive shop in Fujairah. He looked... relaxed. Happy maybe. We talked for ten minutes and he didn't mention his job once except to laugh about how his commute is now a five-minute walk to the beach.

I thought I'd feel sorry for him. Instead I felt something closer to envy and I don't know what to do with that. His life is small by every metric I care about. No status, no visibility, probably makes less than my car payment. But he's not performing anything. He's just there, doing what he wants, and it seems like enough for him.

I can't imagine that being enough for me. And yet the fact that it bothers me is bothering me more.

Everyone keeps asking me why I'm taking on the Chocó expedition when the company is already stretched thin. But they don't understand—this is exactly the kind of opportunity that doesn't come twice. The regional government contacted us specifically. They want sustainable tourism in the Pacific corridor, and we're the ones they trust to do it right.

My sister called to say Mamá is worried I'm "doing too much again." What does that even mean? I've built this company from nothing. Three employees became twenty-two. We survived the pandemic when half the tourism operators in Colombia closed. I know what I'm doing. I'm not going to apologize for wanting to grow, for wanting something more than just coasting.

Nudge: "What would 'coasting' actually look like for you?"

Scouting day in Nuquí went better than expected, the community leaders were receptive, the river routes are navigable. Already picturing the first group coming through in February. This is the part I love—standing somewhere new, seeing what could be.

Back in Bogotá and immediately into the Sunday routine. Made sancocho because that's what Abuela always made in October when the weather turns. Fourteen people around the table today—cousins, their kids, my mother with her opinions about my salt levels. It's chaotic and loud and sometimes I think about how much easier it would be to just... not. To have my Sundays free for once.

But then my nephew Tomás asked me to tell him again about the time I got lost in Leticia and had to barter my watch for a boat ride. He's heard it fifty times. Still loves it. And there's something about seeing the whole family there, passing around the bowls the way we always have, that feels like it matters more than whatever else I could be doing.

Had a moment in the kitchen where I just stood there holding the wooden spoon Abuela used. The same one. It's cracked now but I can't replace it. Weird how objects carry weight like that. Anyway. Good day overall, even if I was tired from the week. The expedition proposal is due Friday and I haven't touched it yet. That's tomorrow's problem.

Sent off the proposal. Clean, professional, ambitious. I asked for twice the funding we technically need because I want a buffer, and I want to bring in local guides from Chocó communities from day one, not as an afterthought. That costs more but it's the right way to do it. My father would have called it impractical—he always wanted me to play it safe, to stay in numbers and spreadsheets.

Thinking about him today. He never understood why I left the firm, never stopped hoping I'd come back. He died still thinking I'd made a mistake. But I think he would have liked seeing the company now, even if he wouldn't admit it. The way we do things matters to me in a way I couldn't articulate when I was younger. Maybe that's his influence after all—just expressed differently.

Nudge: "What would he have noticed first if he saw the company now?"

Response: The order. How organized everything is, the systems I built. He'd pretend not to be impressed but he'd notice. The irony is I use everything he taught me about running a business—I just pointed it at something he never would have chosen. Maybe that's what inheritance actually looks like. You take the tools and build something your parents wouldn't recognize, but couldn't exist without them.

Sunday again. Smaller group today, just eight of us. My mother wasn't feeling well so I drove out to pick her up from Soacha myself. She complained the whole way about the traffic, then about my driving, then about how I never call enough. Classic. But when we got to my place she went straight to the kitchen and started organizing things her way, moving my spices, telling me my onions were too big. And honestly it was fine. More than fine.

There's a rhythm to this. She's seventy-three and still trying to teach me things. I'm forty-eight and still letting her. We don't talk about feelings or whatever, but this is how we do it. She comes, she criticizes my cooking, she eats three helpings, she tells the same story about my uncle Jorge and the goat from 1987.

Started thinking about what happens when she can't make the drive anymore. When the table gets smaller. The company keeps me running in six directions but this—this is the anchor. Nuquí is exciting. The Chocó project could be the biggest thing we've done. But it's not actually about that, is it? It's about having something to come back to. Otherwise you're just moving for the sake of moving.

Nudge: "Does the running ever feel like coming back to something too?"

Response: Sometimes, yes. When I'm navigating a new river or negotiating with a community for the first time—there's a feeling like I'm returning to who I'm supposed to be. Like the version of me that sits still too long starts to decay and the version that moves is more real. Both things can be true I think. The anchor and the current.

Regional government approved the proposal, full funding. I cried a little in my office with the door closed. Texted my sister, called my mother, then spent two hours just staring at topographic maps like a kid planning a treasure hunt.

Nudge: "Tears of what?"

Response: Relief mostly. And maybe grief that Papá isn't here to see it. Complicated tears. The kind where you're happy and sad and proud all at the same time and you can't separate any of it out.

Camilo quit today. Just walked in and said he found something "more stable." Five years with the company and he leaves a month before our biggest project. I don't even know what to feel right now—betrayed, scared, angry, all of it.

Nudge: "What scares you most about losing him?"

Spent the last week scrambling to redistribute Camilo's responsibilities. Ended up giving some of it to María who's only been with us eight months but she's sharp, hungry, reminds me of myself at that age. Told her this is a trial by fire and she just nodded and said "Bueno, vamos." I like that. No drama, just forward motion.

The thing about losing someone is it forces you to see what you've been leaning on. I'd been leaning on Camilo more than I realized—his steadiness, his institutional memory. But the company isn't Camilo. It's bigger than any one person. That includes me, eventually. Something to think about.

Had dinner with my cousin Esperanza who runs a restaurant in Usaquén. She's been doing it for twenty years, same location, same menu basically, same suppliers. I asked her if she ever gets bored. She laughed and said "The food is the same, the people are different. That's enough." I don't know if I could do it. But I respect it. There's honor in tending the same thing year after year. I just don't think it's my kind of honor.

Sunday was Day of the Dead. Set up the ofrenda the way my family always does—photos, marigolds, Papá's favorite rum. Tomás helped me light the candles. He asked if Bisabuelo could really see us from wherever he is. I said I don't know but we do it anyway, because the doing is what matters. That felt true when I said it.

First planning trip to Chocó with the full team next week. Should be excited but I'm mostly anxious—what if the reality doesn't match the vision? What if I've oversold this to everyone including myself?

Nudge: "What would 'matching the vision' actually look like on the ground?"

Response: Community members who see themselves in the project, not just as employees. Routes that feel discovered rather than manufactured. Guests leaving changed. That's the vision. Whether we can actually build that... I guess we'll find out. The gap between dreaming and doing is where I live. Sometimes it feels like flying, sometimes like falling.

Second entry today because I couldn't stop thinking. The anxiety from earlier settled into something more like anticipation. Called my mother to tell her about the trip and she said "Cuídate, mijo, pero también diviértete." Take care, but also have fun. Simple, but it landed.

Nudge: "Why did that land today?"

Response: Because usually she just worries. This time she acknowledged that I need this—the adventure part, not just the work part. Like she finally sees it's not recklessness, it's how I'm built. Took her long enough, but also: took me long enough to stop being defensive about it.

Just back from four days in Chocó. My body is wrecked—humidity, mosquitoes, sleeping on a cot in a community center. But my mind is alive in a way it hasn't been in months. We mapped three potential routes, met with eleven different community contacts, ate more fish and coconut rice than I thought possible.

María was incredible. She handled logistics like she'd been doing this for years. At one point we had a miscommunication with a boat operator and she talked him down in a Chocó dialect I didn't even know she spoke. I told her she has a future here if she wants it. She said "I want it."

The landscape. I keep thinking about the landscape. Rivers that split into a dozen channels, mangroves that go on forever, communities that have lived there for generations doing things their own way. There's a rhythm to life there that we're going to have to respect if any of this works. We can't bring tourists in like we own the place. We have to earn the right to be there, again and again.

Came home to seventeen emails, a broken water heater, and a message from my sister saying Sunday is at her place this week because my nephew has a school thing. Everything keeps moving. That's okay. I know how to move with it now. Or maybe I always did—I just spent too many years feeling guilty about it instead of accepting that this is who I am. Someone who needs both the roots and the river.

Nudge: "When did the guilt start to lift?"

Response: Honestly? Probably when I stopped expecting everyone else to understand it. My mother's blessing last week helped. But mostly I think you just get tired of fighting yourself. The guilt was exhausting. At some point you realize you can honor where you came from and still keep moving. They're not enemies. They never were.

Got the feedback from Dr. Weiss today about the cardiac arrest protocol I revised. He actually read the whole thing. Said it was thorough, well-researched, that he'd pass it to the department head for review. I stood there nodding like it was no big deal but inside I was buzzing. This is the first time anyone senior has taken something I wrote seriously.

I've been working on that protocol for three months. Stayed after shifts, dug through research papers during lunch, ran the numbers on our ward's response times. Most of it I did on my own time because nobody asked me to. I just wanted to prove that I could contribute beyond bedside care. That I'm not just someone who checks vitals and changes IV bags.

After he left I went to the break room and just sat there for a minute. Didn't even get coffee. Just needed a second to let it land. It felt real. Like something I made mattered.

Picked up two extra shifts this week. Covered for Anja on Tuesday and stayed late Thursday for the new grad's first code situation. She froze at the monitors and I had to talk her through it step by step while doing compressions. Afterwards she thanked me and I told her that's just how it goes the first time.

Thomas made a comment about never seeing me anymore. Fair. I've been home maybe four evenings this month. But the ward is short-staffed and I'm trying to show I can handle the load. Being reliable matters when you're trying to move up.

Normal week. Two admissions, one discharge, one transfer to ICU. Ran the morning handoffs all five days since Monika was out sick. Nobody commented on it, which is fine. That's the job.

Had coffee with Klara from the surgical ward. She asked how I was doing since "the thing" — meaning Frau Hartmann getting the charge position instead of me. I said fine. She looked at me like she didn't believe it. I changed the subject to her holiday plans.

Nudge: "What would believing it have looked like?"

Checked the intranet again. Still no updates on the junior ward manager posting. Refreshed it three times during break like that would change anything. Felt stupid afterwards.

Nudge: "Why stupid?"

Response: Just impatient I guess. Nothing unusual.

Thomas said something last night about me always being on my phone even when I'm home. He wasn't wrong, but the way he said it — like I'm doing something wrong by checking work emails — rubbed me the wrong way. I didn't pick a fight about it. Just said okay and put the phone down. But I kept thinking about it.

It's not like I want to be checking emails at 9pm. There's just always something. A shift swap request, a question from one of the students, updates about policy changes. If I don't stay on top of it I fall behind. And falling behind means being invisible.

Nudge: "What happens if you're invisible?"

Response: Then what was the point of any of it. The overtime, the extra certifications, the protocol work. I can't just be average.

Double shift yesterday. Started at 6am, didn't leave until almost midnight. We had a patient crash twice within four hours — Herr Müller, 67, the one with the triple bypass last week. Both times I was the first to respond. Both times we got him back. The second time I couldn't even feel my hands afterwards, just this hollow buzzing in my arms.

Dr. Weiss pulled me aside at the end. I thought he was going to say something about the protocol, maybe some feedback finally. Instead he asked if I was okay. Told me I looked exhausted. That I should take my full breaks. I nodded and said I was fine.

On the U-Bahn home I stared at my reflection in the dark window. Couldn't remember the last time I ate an actual meal during a shift. Or the last time I left on time. There's a performance review coming up next month and I keep thinking if I can just get there, if I can just hold on until then, it'll be worth it. But I don't know anymore.

Nudge: "What would make it worth it?"

Response: The title. The recognition. I don't know, proof that it mattered.

Frau Hartmann asked me to train the new hires on the cardiac monitoring system next week. She said I'm the best on the ward with the equipment and the documentation. Part of me was annoyed — I trained her on half of this stuff two years ago, and now she's delegating it back to me as if she invented it.

But I said yes. What else am I going to say? It's exposure. It's a chance to show the department head what I can do. Even if it means staying late again. Even if Thomas will eat dinner alone again. This is how it works. You don't get anywhere by saying no.

Thomas asked if I wanted to go to his cousin's wedding in August. I said I'd have to check my shifts. He didn't respond. Just went to bed. I stayed up looking at the calendar knowing I could probably make it work but not wanting to commit.

Nudge: "What are you waiting for?"
Finished the training sessions. Got positive feedback from all four new hires. One of them said I made the defibrillator protocols actually make sense. Small win.

Realized I forgot our anniversary was yesterday. Thomas didn't say anything. Just made dinner like normal. I should have remembered. I had a reminder in my phone but I dismissed it during a shift and never looked again.

Nudge: "What did you feel when you realized?"

Performance review scheduled for Friday. Spent the evening updating my self-assessment document. Listed all the overtime, the certifications, the protocol work. Looks good on paper. Should be enough.

Nudge: "Enough for what?"

Advisor asked why I haven't applied to more conferences yet. I know I should be building my CV but lately I just want to curl up with a book that isn't required reading.

Nudge: "What happens if you don't apply?"
Morning coffee at that new kissaten near campus, the one with the jazz records and the old wooden counter. Just sat there for an hour watching the light change through the window. Good start.

Second entry today because I want to remember this. After the coffee shop this morning, I went to the seminar and Professor Mori mentioned a summer research position in Kyoto. It's prestigious, competitive, the kind of thing everyone in the program is scrambling for.

I thought about it during the whole train ride home. The position would mean living in a cramped dorm, working six days a week, minimal stipend. But the name on the CV would be impressive. Meanwhile my friend Emi is planning to spend summer working part-time at a bookshop in her hometown and hiking on her days off.

I kept thinking about what actually sounds like a good summer. Not what looks good, what feels good. Kyoto in summer is humid and exhausting. The dorm has shared bathrooms. I'd be doing research I'm only moderately interested in.

I don't think I'm going to apply. There will be other opportunities. This summer I want to actually enjoy.

Nudge: "What would Emi say about your decision?"

Response: She'd probably be surprised I even considered the Kyoto position at all. Emi has always been better at knowing what she actually wants versus what she thinks she's supposed to want. I've been trained my whole life to chase the next impressive thing. It's only recently I've started asking whether I actually want the things I'm chasing.

Long week. Three seminars, two papers to review for workshop, one very awkward dinner with my mother who flew in from Osaka "just to check in" which means "just to ask about job prospects."

She kept steering the conversation toward practical matters. Have I thought about which companies are hiring? Do I have connections in publishing? What's my backup plan if academia doesn't work out? I just wanted to enjoy the tonkatsu. We were at that place in Shibuya I love, the one with the perfectly crispy cutlets and the house-made pickles, and I could barely taste any of it because I was so busy defending my life choices.

Nudge: "What would that dinner have been like without those questions?"

Response: We would have talked about the food, probably. Mom used to be more relaxed about this stuff. We'd go out to eat and she'd spend the whole time appreciating every dish, teaching me how to notice the details. Somewhere along the way she got more anxious about my future and forgot how to just be present with me. I miss that version of her.

Turned down the Kyoto position officially today. Sent the polite email declining to apply. Felt nothing dramatic, just a small relief.

Spent the afternoon reorganizing my bookshelves by color instead of subject because I wanted my apartment to look beautiful, not efficient. Took about three hours. Could have been writing. Could have been reviewing literature. Instead I arranged my books into a sunset gradient from red to orange to yellow to cream.

My labmate Kenji stopped by to borrow a book and was visibly confused by the arrangement. "How do you find anything?" he asked. I told him I usually remember what color the spine is anyway. He shook his head and muttered something about humanities students.

Made dinner from scratch even though I had instant ramen in the cupboard. Tamagoyaki with rice and miso soup. Sat by my window and watched the city lights come on while I ate. Ordinary evening but somehow exactly what I needed.

Nudge: "What were you recovering from?"

Response: I don't think it was recovery exactly. More like... recalibrating? I've spent so many weeks doing things because I should. The bookshelf thing was completely pointless from a productivity standpoint and that's exactly why it felt good.

Conference deadline week. Everyone in the lab is stressed. The shared office smells like convenience store coffee and desperation.

I finally submitted my abstract at 11pm last night, not because I wanted to but because my advisor made it clear this wasn't optional. The whole process felt hollow. I wrote about a topic I find moderately interesting using frameworks I find mostly tedious to impress reviewers I'll never meet.

The worst part is I used to love this subject. My undergraduate thesis on modernist Japanese literature was something I genuinely cared about. Somewhere in the grad school process the joy got squeezed out. Now it's all about metrics. H-index. Citation counts. Impact factors. Nobody asks if you're enjoying your research. They ask if it's publishable.

Came home at midnight and couldn't sleep. Made hot chocolate and watched old episodes of a cooking show until 3am. Felt guilty about it this morning but honestly it was the happiest I'd been all week.

Nudge: "Why guilty about the happiest part of your week?"

I'm so tired of feeling like enjoying my life is something I need to earn. Like I have to suffer through enough work hours before I'm allowed to have a nice dinner or watch a movie without guilt.

Nudge: "Who taught you that?"

Response: Everyone? No one specific? It's just in the air in East Asian academic culture. Work hard, sacrifice now, enjoy later. My grandmother worked herself to exhaustion running a small restaurant for forty years, always saying she'd rest "when things calm down." She died before they ever did. I don't want that to be my story.

Presented at the workshop today. It went fine. People asked reasonable questions. Advisor said it was "solid work." High praise from him, apparently.

Afterward a few of us went for ramen to decompress. The kind of evening I actually enjoy - warm food, easy conversation, no agenda. One of the third-years asked how I stay so calm about deadlines and I laughed because I don't feel calm at all. I just refuse to let grad school consume every waking moment. They looked at me like I'd said something radical.

Nudge: "What did their reaction make you feel?"

Response: A bit sad, honestly. These are smart, interesting people who have completely bought into the idea that suffering is necessary for success. They've given up hobbies, relationships, sleep - for what? A slightly better chance at a job that might not even exist by the time they graduate? I don't want to be preachy about it but I genuinely think there's another way to do this.

Spent way too much on a nice dinner alone at that French place I've been wanting to try. Felt indulgent. Also felt necessary. Can't explain it.

Nudge: "What made it necessary?"

Response: I think I needed to prove to myself that I can do nice things for myself without justifying it with productivity. No celebration, no milestone, just a Wednesday. I ordered the wine pairing. The waiter didn't ask me why I was dining alone and I was grateful for that.
Advisor wants to meet tomorrow about "trajectory." I know what that means.

The meeting with my advisor went about as expected. He's concerned I'm not "maximizing my potential." Translation: I'm not working enough weekends, not applying to enough conferences, not publishing enough papers. He pointed at his own CV, decades of relentless output, and asked if I understood what it takes.

I told him I understood, but I also told him something I've been thinking about for months. That I watched my grandmother work herself into an early grave. That I've seen colleagues burn out and leave academia bitter and broken. That I want a career, yes, but not at the cost of actually living.

He was quiet for a long time. Then he said something surprising. He told me he missed his daughter's childhood. That his wife almost left him twice. That he sometimes wonders if it was worth it. He said he wouldn't advise anyone to follow his path exactly.

We didn't resolve anything concrete. I still need to publish, still need to be strategic about my career. But for the first time it felt like we were having an honest conversation instead of him just transmitting expectations.

Walked home the long way through the park. The ginkgo trees are that perfect yellow right now. Bought a taiyaki from the street vendor and ate it while it was still warm. Whatever happens with my career, I want to remember that there's a life happening alongside it.

Today was my parents' 40th wedding anniversary, and I spent most of the afternoon at their villa helping coordinate everything. My mother had very specific ideas about the flower arrangements and the seating chart — cousin Layla absolutely cannot be seated near aunt Fatima after what happened at Eid — and I found myself nodding along, rearranging the entire dining room twice to meet her vision.

The funny thing is, I didn't mind. There's something calming about fitting into the role everyone expects. My father pulled me aside after the guests arrived and thanked me for "being the reliable one," and I felt this warmth in my chest. My younger brother Samir flew in from London but spent most of the party on his phone, and I could see the disappointment in my mother's eyes. I don't want to be the source of that look.

But there was this moment, late in the evening, when the older relatives were doing their formal toasts, and I looked across the room at my wife Nadia. She caught my eye and did this little mock-yawn behind her napkin, and I had to bite my cheek not to laugh. We'd both rather have been at that new seafood place on the Palm with a bottle of wine, just the two of us. Instead, I sat through two hours of my uncle's stories about the old days.

I think I'm okay with it. The party was beautiful. Everyone was happy. And Nadia and I are going to that restaurant next weekend anyway — I already made the reservation. You can do both, I think. Be the person your family needs and still carve out space for the things that make life sweet.

I'm so frustrated I could scream. We had our quarterly review today and Hamza — my direct report who I've mentored for two years — threw me under the bus in front of the regional director. He presented his supply chain optimization idea as if it was entirely his own, when we literally developed it together over coffee last month. I was the one who told him to run the numbers on the Oman route!

The worst part is I just sat there. Smiled. Nodded along. When Ahmed, my boss, looked at me after the presentation, I said something supportive about Hamza's initiative. What else was I supposed to do? Create a scene? Interrupt him mid-presentation to claim credit? That's not how things are done. You don't embarrass a colleague publicly, especially one you outrank. It would look petty. Undignified.

But God, I wanted to. I wanted to say, "Actually, that was our idea, developed during our Tuesday coffees." Instead I shook his hand after and told him he did well. He had the grace to look slightly uncomfortable, at least.

Nadia says I should have spoken up privately to Ahmed afterward. She's probably right. But I keep thinking about how my father handled workplace conflicts — with patience, with letting things play out, with trusting that people eventually see the truth. Maybe Hamza will acknowledge it later. Maybe Ahmed already knows whose brain that idea came from.

Or maybe I'm just telling myself stories to feel better about being a coward. I don't know. I do know that I came home and ate an entire box of those pistachio sweets from that place in Sharjah, and I don't regret it one bit. Sometimes you just need something good after a bad day.

Nudge: "You mentioned Nadia's suggestion and your father's approach as two different paths. Which one feels more true to who you want to be?"

Response: Honestly? I think I'm overthinking this. It's one meeting. Hamza is young, he got excited about presenting something that worked. The idea will get implemented either way, and that's what matters for the team. I'd rather focus on the fact that we're on track for a really good Q4, and I'm taking Nadia to Bahrain for New Year's. Some things just aren't worth the energy of picking apart.

Can't sleep. It's 2am and I'm sitting on the balcony with my laptop because Nadia's breathing is usually soothing but tonight it's just reminding me that everyone else can rest except me.

My mother called today and mentioned — casually, as if she was discussing the weather — that cousin Layla's husband just got made partner at his firm and isn't that wonderful, they're buying a bigger house in Abu Dhabi. Then she asked when Nadia and I are going to "settle down properly" and have children. I'm 41. We've been married six years. She knows we've been trying. She knows about the appointments with Dr. Khalil. But she still asks, every single time, like maybe if she reminds me enough, my body will cooperate.

I didn't say anything. I made the appropriate congratulatory noises about Layla's husband. I changed the subject to the marina and the boat engine I'm having fixed.

What I wanted to say was: we might not be able to have children, and it's breaking Nadia apart, and I feel like a failure every time she cries in the bathroom thinking I can't hear her. But you don't burden your mother with things like that. You don't make her worry. You don't disrupt the pleasant fiction that everything is fine and her eldest son has everything figured out.

The thing is, I don't even know if I want children anymore. Is that terrible? I used to. I had the whole picture in my head — teaching my son to fish, taking my daughter to her first day of school. But lately I've been thinking about what our life looks like without them. The freedom. The travel. The late Sunday mornings. Is that selfish? It feels selfish. But it also feels honest.

I should go back to bed. Nadia's got that look lately when I stay up too late, like she's worried about me. I don't want to give her another thing to worry about.

Nudge: "You described the picture you used to have and the one you're starting to imagine now. What would it take to talk about this with Nadia?"

Response: That's the thing — I'm not sure I can. We've never actually said out loud that it might not happen. We talk around it. We talk about appointments, and procedures, and "next steps." But we don't talk about what it means if there are no next steps. I think I'm afraid that if I tell her I'm starting to be okay with the idea of just us, she'll think I've given up on her. On us. On the future she wanted. And I haven't given up. I'm just... adapting? Protecting myself maybe? I don't know. I think I need to figure out what I actually feel before I bring it to her. Right now it's all just noise in my head at 2am.

Bahrain trip was good. Nadia and I finally talked about the fertility stuff. She cried but also seemed sort of relieved, I think? We're going to take a break from the treatments for a few months.

Nudge: "That sounds like a meaningful conversation to have had together. How are you feeling now that it's out in the open?"

Response: You know, mostly I'm just glad to be back at work tomorrow. The holiday period always feels long. We have the Q1 planning meeting on Thursday and I want to make sure the Oman route data is properly credited this time. Fresh start to the year.

Look, I know some teachers think the community garden visits are a waste of instructional time. We spent two hours there today and yes, that's time away from test prep. But watching Marcus Jr. (not named after me, just a coincidence) actually engage with something for the first time all year? Seeing him carefully transplant seedlings and ask questions about composting? That matters.

Mrs. Patterson made a comment in the break room about my "field trip habit" but I'm not going to apologize for it. These kids need to understand they're part of something bigger than standardized tests. They need to touch the dirt, see the earthworms, understand where food actually comes from. That's education too.

Didn't call Grandma back again. She left another voicemail about the restaurant anniversary dinner planning and I just... let it go to voicemail. I'll call her tomorrow.

Nudge: "What made you hesitate to pick up?"

Response: Honestly, I know she's going to ask me to give a speech at the 60th anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. My Cantonese is terrible and I keep meaning to practice but I never do. It feels like I'm letting her down every time we talk and she hears how rusty I've gotten.

Principal wants me to use the new standardized curriculum materials for social studies. They come from the district. They're fine, technically. Cover all the state requirements.

But they're so sanitized. The unit on immigration literally has two paragraphs on the Chinese Exclusion Act. Two paragraphs. My great-grandfather couldn't enter this country for decades because of that law. I told the principal I'd supplement with additional materials. She said to be careful about "going off curriculum." Said it in that way that means don't.

Nudge: "So what will you do with those materials?"

Response: I'm going to use the curriculum. But I'm also going to bring in my family photos and tell the real story during the personal narrative unit. They can't tell me I can't share my own family history. At least I don't think they can.

The kids blew my mind today. We were talking about the water cycle and Jordan raised her hand and asked if the water we drink is the same water dinosaurs drank and I said yes, basically, and then she asked if that meant we were connected to dinosaurs and I said yes again and she got this look on her face like something clicked.

Then Carlos said if we're connected to dinosaurs through water we must be connected to each other too and to the fish and to the trees and I just stood there for a second because yes, exactly, that's exactly it. These ten-year-olds just figured out in five minutes what some adults never understand. We spent the rest of the period talking about interconnection and nobody complained and nobody looked at the clock.

Nudge: "What did that moment mean to you?"

Response: It reminded me why I do this job. Not for the test scores or the evaluations. For moments like that where kids discover something true about the world. That we're all connected, that what we do matters beyond ourselves. That's what I want them to carry with them.

Second entry today because I just need to get this out. Went to the grocery store after school, totally normal trip, and I just stood in the produce section for way too long looking at the plastic clamshells of strawberries. They're from Chile. In January. Wrapped in plastic that's going to exist for five hundred years so I can have strawberries in the middle of winter.

I bought them anyway. I bought the stupid strawberries because I was tired and I wanted something sweet and the farmer's market doesn't open until Saturday. And I know one person buying or not buying strawberries doesn't change the global supply chain but I still felt like a hypocrite putting them in my cart.

This is what I mean when I say it's exhausting to care about everything all the time. My students look at me like I have answers but I'm just as stuck as everyone else. I teach them about sustainability and then I buy Chilean strawberries in plastic. I tell them every choice matters and then I make choices that don't align with that. I don't know how to live in this world without constantly feeling like I'm failing at something.

Called Grandma. We talked for forty minutes. I'm going to give the speech at the anniversary dinner. In Cantonese. She's going to help me practice.

Nudge: "How do you feel about that decision?"

Response: Nervous but good. It's important to her. The restaurant is important to the whole family, to the community really. Dad worked there every summer through high school. So did I. People need to hear what it means to us, in the language it's always been spoken in.

The diversity assembly actually worked! I've been pushing for this for two years and today we had speakers from five different community organizations talking to the whole school. Mrs. Okonkwo from the Nigerian cultural center, Mr. Patel from the Hindu temple, someone from the tribal council, and two others.

The kids were so engaged. They asked real questions. One of my students asked Mrs. Okonkwo what she missed most about Nigeria and she started crying a little and then laughed and said "the food, always the food" and everyone laughed and it was this beautiful human moment. This is what school should feel like. Not test prep and discipline charts. Actual humans connecting with each other, learning that the world is bigger than their own street.

Nudge: "What made you push so hard for this?"

Response: When I was a kid there was nobody who looked like me in the history books or the assemblies. I remember how alone that felt. I don't want any of my students to feel that way. I want them to see themselves in school, and I want them to see each other too.

Made dumplings with Mom today for Lunar New Year prep. My hands remember the folding even when my brain forgets the right way to pleat them. She corrected me three times on the same fold and I wanted to be annoyed but honestly I was just grateful she's still here to correct me.

She told me stories about the restaurant again, the early days when Grandpa worked eighteen hour shifts and Grandma handled the front while raising Dad. Stories I've heard a hundred times but they never get old. There's something about flour on your hands and family around you that makes everything feel solid. Like no matter what else is happening in the world, this is real. This is what matters.

Parent-teacher conferences all day. Twelve families. My voice is gone but one mom thanked me for showing her son that being Chinese-American is something to be proud of. That one sentence made the whole exhausting day worth it.

Nudge: "What did it mean to hear that?"

Response: More than I can explain. I spent so many years feeling caught between two worlds, not Chinese enough, not American enough. If I can help even one kid skip that confusion, help them feel whole from the start... that's why I became a teacher. That's the whole point.

Long week. Just long. A lot of grading, a lot of meetings, a lot of everything. The community garden got a small grant from the city though, so that's something. We can finally fix the broken irrigation system and maybe add a few more raised beds for the school partnership program.

Practiced my speech with Grandma over video call. My Cantonese is getting better but still rough. She says the important thing is not perfect pronunciation but speaking from the heart. I'm trying to believe her. The anniversary dinner is in two weeks and I keep rewriting what I want to say.

Nudge: "What do you keep changing in the speech?"

Response: I keep trying to capture what the restaurant means. Not just to our family but to the town. It's where people celebrated graduations and mourned losses. Where the same families have come for sixty years. It's history. It's proof we belong here. I don't know how to say all that in five minutes, in a language I barely speak, without crying.

I know I should have stayed for the whole faculty meeting but I had to leave early for the speech practice. Principal noticed. She didn't say anything but I saw her write something down. I'll deal with whatever comes from that.

Some things are more important than faculty meetings about parking lot protocols. The anniversary dinner is this Saturday and my grandmother is counting on me. The whole family is counting on me. And maybe that sounds dramatic but the restaurant isn't just a business, it's proof that we made it here, that we stayed, that we built something. I'm not going to let a faculty meeting come between me and honoring that. I'm just not.

Started doing the math again tonight. If I keep the delivery app running five days a week and pick up at least two handyman jobs on the weekends, I can cover everything with about twelve thousand pesos left over. That leftover is supposed to be for the motorcycle insurance renewal in May. I wrote it on a sticky note and put it on the fridge next to the old one that says "DO NOT touch savings."

Funny thing is, I spent Saturday at the parrilla with Cacho and Pocho like always, and the bill came out heavier than usual because we ordered that tira de asado. I didn't blink. Six hours of good food and good talk, what am I going to do, skip it? Some weeks the numbers work, some weeks they don't. But I'm not going to stop living just because the spreadsheet says I should.

My sister Graciela keeps calling to tell me I should go back to a regular job. She doesn't get it. I'm not going to sit in some warehouse again answering to a guy ten years younger than me who treats people like furniture.

Nudge: "What is it about the idea of going back that feels impossible?"

Response: It's not impossible, I just don't want to. There's a difference. Look, I know what I'm doing. I've been managing fine for five years this way. Graciela worries because she's Graciela. She married an accountant, everything in her life has a line item. Not everyone lives like that.

Bad week. The delivery app lowered the per-trip rate again, no warning, no explanation. Just opened the app Monday morning and the numbers were different. I did the same twelve deliveries I always do on a weekday and came home with almost twenty percent less. Twenty percent. That's not nothing. That's a week of groceries, that's Negrita's vet visit I've been putting off.

The handyman side is slower too because people are tightening up. I had three jobs lined up this week and two of them canceled last minute — one lady said she'd "wait until next month" to fix her bathroom faucet and the other one just stopped responding. So now I'm sitting here Thursday night with the calculator app open, moving numbers around like I'm playing some kind of sad puzzle game, trying to figure out where the motorcycle payment comes from.

The thing that really gets to me is the feeling. It's not just about the money, it's the not knowing. Every month is a guess. Every week I wake up and I don't know what I'm going to make. I used to think that was the trade-off for not having a boss breathing down my neck. Lately I'm not so sure the trade-off is worth it. But what am I supposed to do, crawl back and prove Graciela right? No. I'll figure it out. I always figure it out.

Nudge: "You said you're not sure the trade-off is worth it anymore. What would it look like if you really sat with that thought?"

Got a call from Don Alfredo across the street — he needs his whole kitchen faucet replaced plus some tile work. That's a good-sized job, probably two days of work. Felt like I could breathe again for the first time all week.

Motorcycle started making a noise yesterday. A grinding thing when I brake. I took it to Raul's shop and he looked at it and said the brake pads are gone and the disc is warped, it needs replacing. He quoted me a number that wiped out everything I'd saved from the Alfredo job. Every peso.

I stood there in Raul's shop and felt my stomach just drop. Because it's not optional — the motorcycle is how I make deliveries. Without it I'm on foot or taking the subte, which means fewer trips, which means less money, which means next month's rent gets tight. It's like this chain where one thing breaks and everything behind it starts wobbling. I told Raul to do it, because what choice do I have. But I walked home and I couldn't even eat dinner.

Nudge: "When you walked home, what was going through your head?"

Response: Honestly, I was thinking about my old man. He worked at the same factory for thirty-two years. Hated it, but every month the check came in. He never had to wonder if the floor was going to fall out from under him. I used to think that was a sad way to live. Now I'm not so sure. He always had a little envelope in the dresser drawer with cash for emergencies. I don't have that envelope. I don't have anything between me and the next bad surprise. And that thought — that I'm one broken motorcycle or one bad month away from not being able to pay rent — that sat on my chest the whole walk home.

Couldn't sleep. Opened the app at 1am to see if there were any late-night delivery runs. There were two. I took them both, rode the bicycle I borrowed from my neighbor. Came home at 3am with barely enough to cover tomorrow's gas.

Picked up the motorcycle from Raul this morning. It rides clean now, brakes feel solid. That's something. But I'm running on maybe four hours of sleep from the last few days and I can feel it in my eyes, in the way I'm slow to react at intersections. Almost clipped a bus mirror on Corrientes.

Saturday is coming and Cacho already texted about the parrilla. Part of me wants to skip it this week, save the money. But another part of me — the louder part — says that's the one thing that keeps me sane. You take away the asado with friends and what do I have left? A cat, a calculator, and a leaking faucet in my own bathroom that I haven't even bothered to fix.

Nudge: "When you say the parrilla is the one thing that keeps you sane — what does it give you that nothing else does?"

Response: It's the only time during the week where I'm not counting anything. Not counting deliveries, not counting pesos, not counting hours. I sit down, the meat comes, somebody pours the wine, and for a few hours I'm just Roberto having a good time with his friends. Nobody's asking me to fix something or deliver something or be somewhere in fifteen minutes. It's the closest thing I have to feeling like my life is actually mine.

Don Alfredo recommended me to his daughter for some painting work at her new apartment. Three rooms, good rate, she even offered to pay half upfront. I bought Negrita the fancy wet food she likes to celebrate.

Graciela came over unannounced today while I was between deliveries. She brought empanadas, which was nice, except then she sat at my kitchen table and started doing that thing she does where she looks around the apartment cataloguing everything that's wrong. The peeling paint above the window, the chair with the broken leg, the fact that I still don't have a proper desk and eat on the couch.

Then she said — and I quote — "Roberto, you're almost fifty, you can't live like a college student." And I just stood there holding an empanada feeling like I was fourteen again getting lectured. She doesn't see that I actually like my life most of the time. I like picking my own hours. I like riding through Palermo on a Tuesday morning with the sun out and nobody telling me where to be. But she looks at this apartment and she sees failure. And the worst part is that sometimes, when she leaves, I start seeing it through her eyes too.

Nudge: "You said sometimes you see it through her eyes. What do you see when that happens?"

Response: A guy who's been treading water for five years and calling it swimming. Someone who left a stable situation because of pride and never figured out the next step. I see the broken chair and I think, why haven't I fixed that? I'm literally a handyman. And the answer is because when I come home I don't want to fix anything else, I just want to sit down and not move. And that scares me a little because my old man never left things broken. He always had the house perfect. I can't even keep up with my own apartment and I live alone with a cat.

Painting job at Don Alfredo's daughter's place is done. She was happy with it, paid the balance, even tipped me a little extra. So the money situation is stable for the moment. Rent is covered, motorcycle payment is covered, Raul is paid. There's a small cushion again, not much, but it's there.

Went to the parrilla Saturday like always. Pocho brought his nephew, a kid of about twenty-five who does the same delivery app I do but also drives for a rideshare service on the side. He was telling me about his "strategy" — how he works the surge hours, how he tracks which neighborhoods tip best, how he's saving for a down payment on his own place. The whole table was impressed. I just sat there eating my chorizo and feeling old.

It's not jealousy exactly. The kid has energy and optimism and a plan, and good for him. But I was that kid once. I had the energy to work sixteen-hour days and still go dancing with my ex-wife on Saturday night. Now I do eight hours of deliveries and my back locks up. I eat the asado and drink the wine and it's still good, it's always good. But there's this thin layer underneath the enjoyment now, like a film on old coffee. Like I'm enjoying it because I know this might not last. Like I should be saving what I'm spending here but I can't bring myself to stop because this is the part of my life that doesn't feel like survival.

Nudge: "You described a thin layer underneath the enjoyment. When did you first start noticing it?"

Fixed the chair today. The one Graciela noticed. It took me twenty minutes. I don't know why I left it broken for so long. I also patched the peeling paint above the window. Not because she said anything but because once I started with the chair I just kept going. Negrita watched me from the couch like she was supervising.

After I finished I sat in the chair and it felt solid. Stupid thing to feel good about but I did. I texted Cacho and told him I'd bring a bottle of the good Malbec tomorrow — the Catena, not the box wine we usually get. Sometimes you just want to do something a little extra for no reason. I've got the painting money still in my pocket. Next week is uncertain, it always is. But right now the chair is fixed, the wall looks clean, and tomorrow I'll eat well with people I like. That's not nothing.

The faculty review came back today and my department scored highest on the new pedagogical alignment rubric. Twenty-eight years and it still lands when the numbers confirm what you feel in the classroom. I printed the summary and put it in the drawer with the others.

Ran into Katja on the way out, one of the newer teachers I meet on Mondays. She was upset about a parent complaint and I stayed an extra forty minutes walking her through how to frame the follow-up email. Got home late again but Brigitte had already eaten. Didn't say anything about it. I think she's stopped expecting dinner together on school days.

Nudge: "What did Brigitte not saying anything feel like?"

Response: Honestly, worse than if she had complained. When she used to get frustrated I could at least argue I was doing something that mattered. The silence feels like she's decided it's not worth the effort. And I don't know what to do with that because the work genuinely does matter to me, but so does she. I just can't seem to make the hours stretch.

Markus got the state teaching prize nomination. Markus. He's been at the school nine years and I recommended him for the department two years ago because I saw potential. Which is fine, good for him, I even wrote a reference. But I keep circling back to the fact that nobody thought to put my name forward and I've been building this department since before he finished university.

Maybe it's that the committee wants fresh faces. Or maybe my work has become furniture, something everyone relies on but nobody actually sees. I spent an hour tonight reorganising the spreadsheet of student outcomes to see if I'm slipping. I'm not. The numbers are solid. But the numbers don't seem to matter to whoever fills out the nomination form.

Nudge: "You recommended him and he got the nomination. Where does that leave you?"

Monday coffee with Katja, Leon, and Simone. Katja brought a lesson plan she'd redesigned using a framework I introduced two years ago and it was genuinely good. I told her so. Leon asked if I'd review his assessment rubric by Wednesday. Simone needed help navigating a difficult student's parents. I said yes to both without thinking.

Walking home I realised I hadn't touched my own conference paper all week. The submission deadline is in three weeks and I've been telling myself I'd carve out evenings for it. Instead the evenings go to other people's problems. I don't resent them for asking. I resent myself for not being able to say not this week.

Parent evening ran until nine. One father spent twenty minutes questioning why his son got a B+ instead of an A on the Cold War unit and I had to sit there with my folder of rubrics defending a grade I know is fair. This is the part of the job that drains me. Not the teaching, the performing.

Got home and Brigitte asked if I'd remembered to call my sister for her birthday. I hadn't. Sat on the couch for ten minutes not moving before I picked up the phone. Hilde sounded fine but I could tell she noticed I was going through the motions. Another thing I let slide because the school day swallowed everything.

Sunday. Actually managed the whole afternoon without opening the laptop. Brigitte and I drove out to the Eifel, walked a trail we used to do when the kids were small. She pointed out the bench where Thomas fell asleep once, years ago, and we both laughed. It felt like coming back to something.

On the drive home I told her about the conference paper and how behind I am. She said maybe I should let the Monday coffees go for a few weeks. I know she's right in a practical sense. But those three depend on me right now, especially Katja, and I think what I give them matters more than another line on my CV. Or maybe I just tell myself that because it's easier than sitting alone with a half-finished draft.

Nudge: "What would happen if you told them you needed a few weeks?"

Markus won the prize. Everyone clapped at the staff meeting and I clapped too. My hands felt mechanical.

Nudge: "What were you thinking while you clapped?"

Response: That the cake they brought was dry. No, I don't know. It's done, he earned it, I should move on.
Spent the evening reviewing Leon's rubric instead of the conference paper again. It's becoming a pattern I can see clearly but apparently not interrupt.

Submitted a request to attend the European History Educators Conference in June. The head approved it immediately, said I'm long overdue for professional development. That felt decent. It's been four years since my last conference.

Called Hilde properly this time, no agenda, just to talk. She told me about her garden renovation and I actually listened instead of mentally drafting lesson plans. She said I sounded different, more present. I'll take that.

Second thought today. After calling Hilde I sat with my conference paper draft and got two decent pages written. The argument is about how we teach democratic participation as abstract principle rather than practiced skill, and once I started pulling examples from my own classroom it flowed better than I expected.

Brigitte came in with tea around ten and read over my shoulder for a minute. She said it sounded like me at my best, which is the kind of thing she doesn't say often enough for me to take it lightly. I saved the file and actually closed the laptop before midnight. Small accomplishment but I'll count it.

Nudge: "What made tonight different from the other evenings?"

Monday coffee again. This time I told the three of them I'd need to scale back for the next two weeks because of the paper deadline. Katja immediately said of course, Leon nodded, Simone looked a bit lost but said she understood. Nobody collapsed. Nobody resented it, at least not visibly.

Walking home I felt lighter than I have in weeks but also oddly guilty, as though I'd broken an unspoken contract. Brigitte would say that contract only exists in my head. She'd probably be right. I sat down with the draft again after dinner and got another page done. The structure is taking shape.

Nudge: "Where does that unspoken contract come from?"

Response: My father, probably. He was a teacher too, and his door was always open. Students, colleagues, neighbours. I grew up believing that being good at what you do means being available to everyone who needs you. The problem is you can spend a whole career being available and still feel like you haven't done the thing you were actually capable of. I don't want to retire with a drawer full of other people's thank-you cards and an empty shelf where my own work should have been.

Brigitte said something last night that I keep turning over. We were in the kitchen and she mentioned, casually, that she'd stopped inviting the Fischers for dinner because I always cancel or sit there half-present checking my phone for student emails. I started to argue but she cut me off and said she wasn't angry, just adjusting. That word, adjusting, hit harder than any argument.

I've been telling myself the work matters, and it does. The department results prove it, the younger teachers tell me constantly how much the Monday sessions help. But Brigitte adjusting means she's already factored in my absence as the default. My sister noticed the same thing on the phone last week. I don't think I'm neglecting them on purpose. It's more that every time I choose between something with a measurable outcome and something that just asks me to be present, I pick the one I can point to later and say, see, this counted.

I don't know when being present stopped counting for me. Or if it ever did count and I just never built the habit of valuing it properly. The conference paper is going well now and I should feel satisfied. But Brigitte adjusting is sitting in my chest like a stone and I can't spreadsheet my way out of it.

Another sprint planning meeting where they want me to estimate the same maintenance tickets I have been doing for three years. I know exactly how long everything takes now. That is maybe the problem.

Nudge: "What's bothering you about knowing?"

The new architect they brought in, Jennifer, she wants to rewrite the payment gateway from scratch. Using Go instead of Java. Everyone in the room looked at me like I was supposed to object, since I built the original thing in 2019. But honestly? Part of me wanted to say yes, let us do it, let me learn something new instead of patching the same codebase forever.

I did not say that though. I said we should consider the timeline and the risk to the December release. Which is true. The business needs stability. But I walked out of that meeting feeling like I had swallowed something heavy.

Nudge: "You sided against yourself in there?"

Response: It is not that simple. Jennifer does not know what she does not know about the regulatory requirements. Someone had to be the adult.

Elena surprised me with tickets to a tech conference in San Francisco next month. Not the corporate kind with vendor booths and networking bingo, but one of those unconference events where people just propose sessions in the morning and see what happens. She said she noticed I have been looking at conference websites lately but never registering for anything.

I should be excited. Five years ago I would have booked the flight before she finished her sentence. Now I am already thinking about the sprint commitments, about whether Marcus can handle the on-call rotation alone, about what my manager will say when I request the days off. The conference is on a weekend. I would only need to take Friday off. Still, I hesitated.

What bothers me most is not the hesitation itself. It is that I used to make decisions faster. Move to Austin? Two weeks to decide. Take the startup job over the stable bank position? One evening with Elena and a bottle of wine. Now I research, I weigh, I consider all stakeholders. My mother would say I finally grew up. But it feels more like something got dimmer.

I will go to the conference. I already told Elena yes. I just wish the yes had come easier.

Nudge: "When did decisions start feeling heavy?"

Response: Maybe around the time my father got sick. 2018. Suddenly there were consequences everywhere I looked. Every choice felt connected to whether I would be there, whether I was reliable. He passed in 2019 and I thought it would lift, but it did not. The weight just found new things to attach to.

Long day. Production incident at 3 AM, the payment gateway again. Not the part Jennifer wanted to rewrite, ironically, but the legacy integration with the bank's SFTP server. I was on call so I got the page. Took four hours to trace it to a certificate expiration that nobody had on their calendar.

By 7 AM I had fixed it and documented everything. By 8 AM I was in the standup explaining what happened. By 9 AM I was getting messages from the VP asking how we let this happen. Not thank you for catching it. Not good job on the quick fix. Just how did we let this happen.

I did not let anything happen. I inherited this system. I have been asking for budget to modernize the certificate management for two years. But I did not say that either. I just said we would add better monitoring and put the renewal dates in a shared calendar. Safe answer. Team player answer.

Elena asked me at dinner why I looked so tired. I said work was busy. I did not tell her about the VP message because then she would get angry on my behalf and I would have to either agree with her anger or defend the company that made me feel small today. Both options felt exhausting.

I need to sleep but my mind keeps rehearsing the incident like I missed something. I did not miss anything. The certificate was not in any documentation I had access to. But I keep checking anyway.

Nudge: "You keep swallowing things down lately."

Response: Maybe I do. It is easier than the alternative. Every time I push back, I become the difficult one, the senior engineer who cannot adapt, the old guy who thinks he knows better. So I absorb it. I fix things quietly. I make myself small so the team can function. It is what my father did at his factory job for forty years. I used to think he was weak for that. Now I understand he was just tired.

The morning after a bad night is always strange. Everything looks normal. Coffee tastes the same. I just feel like I am watching myself go through the motions from somewhere slightly outside.

Nudge: "What motions are you watching yourself do?"

Response: The usual routine. Checking Slack before I get out of bed. Reviewing the overnight alerts. Logging into the standup. Answering questions I have answered a hundred times. It is not bad, exactly. It is just automatic. Like I am not really needed there, just my pattern.

Flew to San Francisco yesterday for the unconference. Staying at a hotel near the Embarcadero with a view of the bay. Weather is cold and gray but I brought my good jacket.

The conference format is interesting. You show up in the morning, people pitch sessions on index cards, everyone votes with stickers, and then the schedule emerges. No predetermined speakers, no approved slides. Someone proposed a session on rebuilding legacy systems with LLMs. Someone else wanted to discuss leaving tech after 50. I almost proposed something about maintaining software versus maintaining yourself, but I did not have the index card ready in time.

I attended four sessions. The legacy systems one was good, practical advice I could use. The leaving tech one was harder to sit through. A woman around my age talked about how she realized she had optimized her whole life for a company that laid her off in an afternoon. She cried a little. Everyone was respectful.

Tonight I walked along the waterfront instead of joining the dinner group. Watched the ferries come and go. Called my mother. She asked if I was eating well and I said yes, even though I had skipped lunch. Some lies are kindness.

Tomorrow is the last day. I feel different here. Not better or worse. Just different.

Nudge: "What stopped you with that index card?"

Response: I was still figuring out what I wanted to say. It felt too personal for a room of strangers. Maybe next time.

Back from the conference for a week now and people at work keep asking how it was. I say it was great, very inspiring, lots of good ideas. Which is true. But when they ask what I am going to do differently, I do not have a good answer. Learn Go? Propose a rewrite? Those feel like someone else's moves.

My manager suggested I do a lunch-and-learn to share what I learned. I said I would think about it. I probably will not do it. Public speaking was never my thing, and honestly, the sessions that meant most to me were the ones I cannot really present in a corporate setting. Nobody wants a slide deck about existential uncertainty in late-career engineers.

Nudge: "What did you actually bring back from there?"

Response: Some business card contacts. A reading list. The usual conference stuff.

December sprint started with three people out sick and a scope increase nobody agreed to. I have been working ten-hour days trying to keep us on track for the holiday release. Jennifer is frustrated because her Go rewrite keeps getting deprioritized. I am frustrated because I am the one who has to explain why to her without making leadership look bad.

Had dinner with Elena's cousin Miguel who is visiting from Monterrey. He runs a small construction company there.
