He asked me about my job and I tried to explain sprint planning and stakeholder management and he just laughed and said it sounds like I spend a lot of energy managing people's feelings about work instead of actually working. He did not mean it unkindly but it stuck with me. Maybe he is right. Maybe that is what corporate software engineering becomes after a while.

Nudge: "What would actually working look like for you?"

Response: Building something. Seeing it run. Not writing status reports about why it is not done yet. But that is not realistic at my level, I suppose.

I finally did it. I proposed to my manager that I move to Jennifer's team for the Go rewrite project. Not as a lead, not as an architect. Just as a developer. Someone who writes code and learns the new language and maybe remembers what it felt like to not know everything about a codebase.

He looked at me like I had asked for a demotion. Which, technically, I had. He said he would need to think about it, talk to HR, consider the implications. He asked if everything was okay at home. I said yes, everything is fine, I just want to learn something new before I am too old to learn.

I do not know why I said it like that. I am not too old. But something in me wanted to say it out loud, the fear underneath. Like naming it would make it less powerful.

Elena was proud of me when I told her. She said she has been waiting for me to do something like this for years. I did not know she was waiting. I thought I was hiding the restlessness well enough. Apparently I was only hiding it from myself.

Now I wait to see what they decide. Maybe they say no and I stay in my maintenance role forever. Maybe they say yes and I flounder in a new language at 57. Both possibilities feel more alive than certainty.
They said yes. Contingent on finishing the holiday release first, but yes. I start on Jennifer's team in January. I have been studying Go tutorials at night, feeling like a student again.

So I just got back from the department mixer and my head is swimming with possibilities. Priya from the neuroscience lab was talking about this crazy project they're starting with VR and neural mapping and I kept thinking why am I not doing THAT. My current project is fine, it's perfectly fine, it's just—sequencing data, more sequencing data, writing scripts to process sequencing data. Rinse and repeat for the next three years apparently.

Then again I did just switch labs six months ago so maybe I should stay put? But Dr. Mehta's stuff is so much more interesting. He was at the mixer too and mentioned they're doing fieldwork in Costa Rica next semester. COSTA RICA. I haven't been anywhere since Vietnam and my feet are literally itching.

I talked to Aditya after and he thinks I'm insane. "Bro you can't switch labs again, what will your committee think?" He's probably right. He's always right and it's annoying. But also he's been in the same lab since day one doing the same protein folding stuff and seems perfectly happy which I genuinely don't understand. How does someone just... do one thing?

Anyway the mixer had good samosas at least. Small wins.

Nudge: "What's the Costa Rica project actually about?"

Response: Honestly? I don't even fully know. Something about tracking disease vectors in rainforest ecosystems. I think what got me was just... the image of it. Being in a rainforest with equipment, collecting real samples, not just sitting in front of a terminal. I've been in climate-controlled rooms for two years straight. Maybe it's not about the science at all, maybe I just need to feel like I'm actually DOING something with my body, not just my brain. That's probably a terrible reason to uproot everything again.

I can't believe my parents called during lab meeting. Like yes I know I told them to call anytime but I didn't mean DURING LAB MEETING. Had to decline and now I have three missed calls and a WhatsApp message that just says "call us urgent" which probably means they found out about the Vietnam credit card bill I thought I'd hidden.

But that's not even the real issue. The real issue is that Dr. Sharma asked me about my thesis timeline today and I had nothing to say. Literally nothing. I don't know what my thesis IS yet. Everyone else seems to have these perfectly formed research questions and I'm over here like "I don't know, something with genomics maybe? Can I take a gap year?" Obviously I didn't say that last part out loud.

The worst part is I know I'm smart enough to do this. I know I could focus if I wanted to. But I sit down to work on my analysis and within twenty minutes I'm looking at flights to New Zealand or researching pottery classes or wondering if I should learn to surf. My brain just doesn't want to stay in one place. Sometimes I feel like there's something wrong with me, like everyone else got a stability gene I missed.

Amma's going to be so disappointed when she finds out I haven't figured anything out. She keeps telling everyone back home that her son is getting a PhD in America. What she doesn't say is that her son might not actually finish it.

Nudge: "That stability gene thing—do you actually believe that?"

Response: I don't know. Sometimes. Like when I watch Aditya work on the same problem for eight hours straight and I can barely sit still for forty-five minutes without needing to get up, get chai, check my phone, look out the window. It makes me feel broken. But then I also think—maybe I'm just not doing the right thing? Like maybe if I found the RIGHT project I'd be obsessed with it? I've been obsessed before. I once spent three straight days learning to solve a Rubik's cube when I was fifteen, didn't eat properly, didn't sleep. So it's possible. I just haven't found the research equivalent of that yet.

Okay so something weird happened today. I was in the library trying to read papers and I got a LinkedIn message from this startup in Singapore. They saw my GitHub and want to interview me for a bioinformatics engineer role. Not a PhD program, just a job. With a salary. And the office is in Singapore which—I've never been to Singapore.

I know I should just delete it. I'm in the middle of a PhD. You don't just leave a PhD for a random job opportunity. That's what my parents would say. That's what Sharma would say. That's what everyone would say.

But I haven't deleted it yet. It's just sitting there in my inbox like a little door that's cracked open. The company does something with personalized medicine and CRISPR delivery. Actually kind of interesting? I watched their YT videos for like an hour instead of reading papers. The office has this rooftop garden and the team seems young and the founder used to be at MIT before she moved back to Asia.

I messaged Rani about it—she's my cousin in Mumbai who actually works in tech—and she was like "bro at least do the interview, what do you have to lose?" And I was like "my entire academic career?" And she was like "your career isn't a vase, it won't break if you explore options."

I don't know. I'm probably making this into a bigger thing than it is. It's just one LinkedIn message.

Nudge: "You spent an hour on their videos—what kept you watching?"

Response: I think it was the founder's story actually. She was in a top PhD program, left after her second year, started this company, and now they're actually helping people? Like real patients. Not in ten years when a paper gets published and maybe influences a treatment, but NOW. She kept talking about "velocity" in one of the videos—how fast they move from idea to implementation. And I realized I've never experienced that. Academia is so slow. Everything takes years. Peer review takes months. I feel like I'm moving through honey while the rest of the world is running. Maybe I'm just impatient. Maybe impatience is a character flaw I need to fix. Or maybe... I'm in the wrong environment.

I did the interview. First round, just a thirty-minute video call with their head of engineering. I was so nervous I almost backed out twice but Rani talked me off the ledge. And it went... well? Like, really well? She asked me about my GitHub projects and actually seemed interested in the weird evolutionary algorithm thing I built for fun. In my PhD no one cares about that stuff, it's not "publishable."

She mentioned they're building something new—can't share details yet—but they need someone who can "think sideways." I've never been told I think sideways as a compliment before. Usually it's more like "Arjun, can you stay focused on the problem at hand?" There's a second round next week. I haven't told anyone except Rani.

Nudge: "Why keep it secret?"

Response: Because the moment I tell someone in the program, it becomes real. And then I'll have to defend it. Explain it. Justify it. And I don't know if I can. How do I explain to Dr. Sharma that I might leave his lab—the lab I specifically transferred into six months ago—for a startup job? He'll think I'm not serious. Everyone will think I'm not serious. And maybe they're right? Maybe I'm just chasing the next shiny thing again and this isn't any different from switching labs or going to Vietnam or any of the other times I've... anyway. One interview at a time.

Second interview scheduled. My heart is doing weird things. Also I definitely bombed my committee meeting today because I couldn't focus but whatever.

Nudge: "What happened in the committee meeting?"

Timeline of the past week:
- Monday: Second round interview. Went four hours. Met six people. Took a technical assessment that was actually fun.
- Tuesday: Couldn't focus on lab work. Kept checking email.
- Wednesday: They asked for references. Panicked. Gave Rani's contact and my undergrad advisor.
- Thursday: Dr. Sharma asked if everything was okay. Apparently I've been "distracted." Lied and said family stuff.
- Friday: Offer came in.

They want me to start in January. Salary is more than my stipend obviously. Relocation assistance. Singapore work visa they'll sponsor. The role is basically building their data pipeline from scratch.

I haven't responded yet. I have until next Friday. I've been sitting in my apartment for three hours just staring at the wall. I called Amma and she asked about my research and I said it was going great. I'm a terrible son.

Facts I'm trying to process:
1. If I leave, I won't have a PhD.
2. If I stay, I might still not get a PhD (dropout rates are real).
3. Singapore is 13 hours by flight from Bangalore. The US is 22 hours.
4. I've never lived in Southeast Asia long-term but I loved traveling there.
5. My apartment lease ends in January anyway.
6. I don't know what I want.

Nudge: "Forget the facts for a sec—what's your gut say?"

Response: Gut is overrated. Gut is what made me switch labs twice. Gut is what made me book a flight to Hanoi three hours after a breakup. My gut has historically terrible judgment and everyone in my life would agree. Maybe that's the problem—I've always followed my gut and look where it got me: 24 years old, no finished degrees past undergrad, no savings, and a LinkedIn message away from blowing up my life again.

Entry number two today because I can't shut my brain off. Went for a walk around campus and ended up sitting by the fountain for like two hours. This undergrad came up and asked if I was okay and I was like "yeah just having a quarter-life crisis, normal stuff."

The thing is—and I've been avoiding thinking about this—the thing is that even if Singapore goes badly, even if it's a huge mistake, at least I'll KNOW. I'll know what it's like to work at a startup. I'll know what it's like to live in Singapore. I'll have that data. Right now I'm operating on pure hypothesis. "Maybe academia isn't for me" is a theory, not a fact. The only way to get facts is to run the experiment.

But experiments have costs. If I leave and it fails, I can't just come back and pick up where I left off. The PhD window closes. And I'm not sure I can start over again at 26 or 27. There's something about the timing that feels like now or never.

Aditya texted me asking if I wanted to grab dinner. I said I was busy. I've been saying I'm busy a lot lately. I think he knows something's up. I'm a terrible friend and a terrible son and maybe just a terrible person who can't commit to anything or anyone.

Nudge: "Terrible person for... considering options?"

Response: When you put it like that it sounds ridiculous. But it's not just this one decision, it's the pattern. It's always something with me. New city, new project, new plan. My parents have been patient for years and I keep changing directions. Aditya sticks with things. My cousin Rohan finished his MBA in two years flat, married his college girlfriend, stable job at Deloitte. Everyone else seems to have figured out how to commit and I'm over here treating my life like a buffet, sampling everything, finishing nothing. At some point that stops being "exploration" and starts being "avoidance," right?

Third entry today. Decided to accept the offer. Sent the email. Going to Singapore in January.

Nudge: "How do you feel right now?"

Told Dr. Sharma yesterday. He was... actually really nice about it? I expected disappointment or anger but he just said "Arjun, you've always seemed like you were searching for something. I hope you find it." Which made me cry a little bit, not gonna lie. He said if I ever want to come back to academia, he'd write me a letter. That was generous. More generous than I deserved probably.

Told Amma and Papa last night. That was harder. Amma went quiet for a long time and then said "so no PhD?" and I said "not right now" and she said "okay" and passed the phone to Papa who talked about health insurance for twenty minutes. I think it's their way of processing. They didn't yell which is something. Rohan apparently defended me—said tech is where the money is anyway, maybe this is smart. I'll take whatever allies I can get.

Now I'm in this weird in-between space. Still going to lab every day because technically I haven't withdrawn yet, but everyone knows. Aditya hugged me when I told him and said "you crazy bastard, I knew it." We got biriyani delivered and he didn't even lecture me about being impulsive which is growth for him.

I'm scared. I should say that. I'm really scared. But also there's this fizzy feeling in my chest that I haven't felt in a long time. Like when I booked the Vietnam trip, or when I switched to Sharma's lab. It's the feeling of something NEW coming. I know that feeling can't be trusted completely but god it's good when it's here.

Nudge: "What makes that feeling untrustworthy?"

Response: History, I guess? Every time I feel like this, I tell myself "this is it, this is the thing, this will be different." And then six months later or a year later I'm feeling stuck again and looking for the next exit. So I don't know if this is genuine alignment with what I want or just my brain's addiction to novelty dressed up as insight. But I guess I'll find out. Worst case, I get a year in Singapore, learn some stuff, and figure out my next move. Best case... I don't know what best case looks like yet. That's kind of exciting too.

Packing is harder than I thought. Every item is a decision. Also found my old journal from undergrad where I wrote "I will definitely finish my PhD" in all caps.

Nudge: "That undergrad version of you—what would he think?"

Response: Probably that I'm a flake. But also that kid had never tried and failed at anything substantial yet. He didn't know what two years of incremental research feels like. He just knew PhDs sounded impressive. I'm trying not to be too hard on either version of myself—the one who wrote that and the one who's walking away from it now. We're both just trying to figure it out.

People keep asking if I'm "sure" about this. Yes I'm sure. I mean, I signed the contract, booked the flight, told everyone—what else is "sure" supposed to look like?

Nudge: "Is anyone asking that you haven't already asked yourself?"

Response: No. That's the thing. I've asked myself a thousand times. Am I running away? Am I being impulsive? Am I disappointing everyone? What if I hate it? What if I'm just fundamentally incapable of staying anywhere? I've interrogated this decision from every angle. But at some point you just have to move. Uncertainty doesn't go away by thinking harder. You have to actually live through the thing to know if it was right. So yeah, I'm sure—not sure it's the right choice, but sure that I need to make A choice. Staying in a PhD I'm not committed to isn't safety, it's slow suffocation. Whatever Singapore is, at least it'll be something different.

Can't sleep. Kept redoing the sketch for the Suba district mural because the committee wants something "uplifting" and every version I draw keeps drifting toward the river contamination. My hand knows what it wants to make but the brief says otherwise.

Nudge: "What would happen if you showed them your real sketch?"

Response: They'd smile politely and tell me to try again. That's how these things work — you read the room, you adjust, you keep the project alive. It's not about my sketch.

Good day at the workshop. One of the teenagers, David, showed up with a collage about his neighborhood flooding last year — garbage and river water mixed together. He made it beautiful without sanitizing it. I wish I had his nerve sometimes.

Nudge: "What do you think gives him that nerve that you wish you had?"

Response: He doesn't have a committee reviewing his work. He doesn't have a landlord or a mother who calls every Sunday asking if the grants are coming through. David just has cardboard and glue and something he needs to say. There's a freedom in that. I used to have it too, I think, before I learned all the reasons to hold back.

Meeting with Patricia from the environmental collective went better than expected. She's organizing a march next month about the deforestation in Guaviare and wants muralists to paint temporary pieces along the route. No committee, no brief, no polite rewrites — just paint what you see and feel. I said yes before I finished my coffee.

Spent the afternoon looking at satellite images of the cleared areas. It's staggering how fast the green disappears between one photo and the next. I started sketching a piece that layers the satellite view underneath a portrait of a family I met two years ago when I traveled there for a residency. They had this small farm at the edge of the forest. I keep wondering if that farm still exists.

The march is February 28. Patricia asked if I'd also speak at the rally beforehand. I told her I'd think about it. Speaking is different from painting — you can't hide behind brushstrokes. My mother would probably tell me to just do the mural and not draw attention to myself. She still worries about artists getting into trouble. But Patricia's right that the work means more when the person behind it shows up.

The Suba mural committee approved a revised design. I compromised — kept the river but softened the contamination imagery into something more abstract. Flowing blues and greens instead of the murky brown I originally wanted. They loved it. Said it was "inspiring." I nodded and thanked everyone and then sat in my car for ten minutes staring at the steering wheel.

I keep telling myself this is how it works. Public art is negotiation. You serve the community's vision, not just your own. And maybe the abstracted version will still land — maybe someone will look at those blues and sense that something is wrong underneath. That's the hope, anyway. But part of me keeps hearing David's collage in my head, all that directness, no translation required.

Called my cousin Marcela tonight. She's a lawyer in Medellin, very proper, always knows the right thing to say at family dinners. I told her about the march and she got quiet for a long time. Then she said, "Lu, you know how tia Rosa talks. Just be careful what you attach your name to." And I know she means it with love. I know the family worries. But careful has a cost too, and I'm starting to wonder how much it's adding up.

Nudge: "When you say careful has a cost — what does that cost feel like day to day?"

Response: Like a low hum. This background noise of things I chose not to say, images I softened, opinions I kept to dinner with close friends. None of it is dramatic on its own. But when I look at my portfolio from five years ago versus now, the work is technically better but quieter. The edges got filed down somewhere. I'm not sure when it happened exactly.

Family group chat exploded today. My uncle Hernando posted a news article about the Guaviare deforestation — same issue the march is about — and wrote "sad but what can you do, these people need to eat." I started typing a response three times. Deleted all of them.

It's not that I disagree entirely — the economics of it are real and I've seen the poverty firsthand. But the framing drives me insane. "What can you do" as if there are no choices, no policies, no one responsible. I wanted to write about the logging companies, the lack of enforcement, the families who actually live there being displaced. But Hernando is my mother's brother and I can already picture the awkward silence at Easter dinner if I go off in the group chat.

So I sent a sad emoji and closed the app. Mature. Professional. Utterly hollow. Patricia would have posted a five-paragraph essay with citations. I admire that and also cannot imagine being that person in my own family. The mural for the march — that I can do. Words in the chat where my grandmother can see them — that's different somehow, and I hate that it's different.

Finished the base layer of the march mural today. It's honest work. Stood back and felt something I haven't felt in a while — that the paint is saying exactly what I mean.

Nudge: "Sounds like something clicked. What's different about this piece?"

My mother called. Someone told her about the march mural. She didn't yell — she never yells — she just got very quiet and asked "is this going to be a problem for you?" That question does more damage than yelling ever could.

Nudge: "What does her worry do to you?"

Response: Makes me want to reassure her, mostly. I told her it's just a painting, nothing political, which is not entirely true. But she doesn't need to carry that. She has enough.
Long day prepping supplies for Friday. Shoulders ache. Didn't think about the committee or my mother or the march. Just mixed pigments and cleaned brushes. Sometimes that's enough.

Two days until the march. Patricia confirmed I'm on the speaker list. I told her yes three weeks ago and have been quietly hoping she'd forget or the schedule would change. It didn't. So now I'm sitting here at midnight trying to figure out what to say to a crowd when I couldn't even type a paragraph in my family's group chat.

The mural is done. I'm proud of it — really proud. It shows the forest canopy dissolving into ash at the edges, with small figures of families walking along the border between green and grey. It's direct without being preachy, I think. But speaking is a different exposure. My name attached to words, recorded on someone's phone, shared. My uncle could see it. My mother definitely would hear about it. Marcela's voice keeps playing in my head: "be careful what you attach your name to."

I keep going back and forth. One minute I think: this matters more than anyone's comfort, including mine. The next minute I think: who am I to stand on a stage when I can't even push back on a mural committee? Maybe I should just let the painting speak and stay behind it where I'm good. But Patricia asked me specifically because she said artists need to be visible, not just their work. And she's not wrong. I just wish being visible didn't feel like standing in traffic.

Nudge: "If you speak and your family hears — what's the worst that actually happens?"

The march was last Friday. I spoke. It was short — maybe three minutes — and my voice shook the entire time. I talked about how art can hold what policy reports can't, the texture of a place disappearing, the face of someone losing their home to a decision made in an office they'll never enter. Patricia squeezed my arm when I sat down. The crowd was bigger than I expected.

My mother hasn't mentioned it. Marcela sent a one-word text: "brave." I don't know if that's approval or a warning. Hernando liked a photo of the mural on Patricia's Instagram, which is confusing. The sky didn't fall. The family didn't fracture. I feel lighter than I have in weeks, and also a little raw, like a layer of skin is missing. Not sure if I'd do it again. But I'm glad I did it once.

Nudge: "What surprised you most about how it felt?"

Response: That nobody was angry. I'd built this whole catastrophe in my head — my mother crying, my uncle calling me irresponsible, the family closing ranks. None of it happened. The worst outcome was silence, and silence turned out to be bearable. The actual surprise was that the shaking in my voice didn't make me less credible. People came up to me afterward and said it made them listen harder. I don't know what to do with that yet.

Same day, later. The Suba committee emailed asking if I'd be interested in a second mural for a park renovation. They mentioned they saw "my recent public work" and want something with that "raw quality." I read the email four times.

On one hand — this is exactly what I wanted. Someone seeing the unfiltered version and asking for more of it. The kind of work where I don't have to translate myself into something palatable. But then I read the rest of the email and there are guidelines, approval stages, a heritage review board. The same machinery. And I started wondering whether they want the rawness or the idea of rawness, something that looks honest but still fits neatly into a park renovation plan.

I haven't replied yet. Part of me wants to say yes and fight for the real version from inside the process. Part of me wants to say no and keep doing the kind of work where I answer only to the paint. And there's a third part — the loudest one, honestly — that says: just be grateful someone asked, don't be difficult, say thank you and show up and do good work within the lines. That voice sounds a lot like my mother. I don't think she's wrong. I don't think she's entirely right either. Going to sleep on it.

Met with the Rotterdam curator today. She spent twenty minutes explaining the "cohesive vision" for the group show and showed me examples of what she's looking for from other artists. Clean lines, muted palettes, conceptual restraint. Everything I've been moving away from in my studio work.

I nodded along and told her I had some pieces in development that would fit perfectly. I don't. I have canvases full of saturated color and texture that would look completely wrong next to what she described. But this is the Kunsthal feeder gallery — a good showing here means better representation, better sales, a step closer to the kind of career I've been working toward for eight years. So I'll make something new. That's what professionals do.

Started three new sketches for Rotterdam. Muted greens, geometric forms, nothing that feels like mine but everything that fits. Deadline is October 15th. Plenty of time if I stay focused.

Nudge: "What makes something feel like yours?"

Response: Ownership is complicated in this industry.

My friend Sanne came by the studio today and asked why everything looked so different. I told her about Rotterdam and she got this look — not disappointed exactly, more like she was watching me make a choice she wouldn't make. She said "you could always say no" like it was simple. Like turning down the Kunsthal feeder show wouldn't ripple through every networking dinner and studio visit for the next two years. Like my gallerist wouldn't quietly wonder if I'm "difficult."

The thing is, I remember being 24 and thinking I'd never compromise on my work. I had this whole philosophy about integrity and staying true to my vision. But then you go to enough openings where the artists with gallery support have catering and yours has boxed wine you bought yourself. You watch people with half your skill get collected because they know how to make curators feel smart. You learn that the art world runs on relationships, and relationships run on making people comfortable.

I'm not selling out. I'm participating in a system that exists whether I like it or not. Sanne can afford her principles because her parents own three properties in Utrecht.

Nudge: "You're explaining why it makes sense. I'm curious if it feels right."

Response: No. It doesn't feel right. It feels like I'm building a portfolio that looks like success while my actual work stays hidden in a corner of my studio. But "feeling right" hasn't paid my rent in eight years — meeting expectations has. I don't know how to want different things than I want.
Worked twelve hours on the Rotterdam pieces. They're competent. That's the worst word I could use. Competent.

Quarterly review with my gallerist Pieter this morning. He went through the sales figures, upcoming opportunities, collectors who've expressed interest. I've sold more this year than last year. I should feel proud of that.

He mentioned Rotterdam and said it's "exactly the kind of strategic move" that builds long-term careers. He used the word "strategic" three times. I wanted to tell him that I've been sneaking into my studio at night to work on my own pieces — the ones that won't sell, the ones that feel alive — but instead I thanked him for his guidance and asked about the fair calendar for next spring.

Nudge: "What happens in those night sessions?"

Response: I paint without thinking about who's going to see it. Thick impasto, colors that clash, marks that look unfinished. It's probably worse than my commercial work technically. But I lose track of time when I'm doing it. That hasn't happened with the Rotterdam pieces once.

Got coffee with two artists from my residency cohort — Anke and Willem. Willem just turned down a show because the venue wanted him to "tone down the political elements." Anke nodded like this was obviously the right call. I sat there wondering if I'm the only one who does the math before every decision.

When I mentioned Rotterdam they both went quiet for a moment before Anke said "that's great for you" in a voice that clearly meant something else. I'm tired of being the person who plays by the rules in a room full of people who act like rules are optional. Some of us don't have trust funds or teaching positions or partners with stable incomes. Some of us need our art to actually function as a career.

I didn't say any of that. I just said I was excited about the opportunity and let them judge me silently over their flat whites.

Can't sleep again. Keep thinking about this conversation I had with my mother last week. She asked how the career was going and I listed my accomplishments like a CV and she said she was proud but then asked if I was happy and I said yes immediately without thinking about it. Was that a lie? I'm successful by every metric I set for myself ten years ago. Gallery representation before 30. Consistent sales. Press coverage. A studio I can afford. That's supposed to be the goal.

But then I think about the night sessions and how they're the only time I feel like I'm actually doing something instead of performing something and I don't know what to do with that. If I follow that feeling I lose everything I've built. If I ignore it I keep building something that looks like a life from the outside.

Nudge: "What if it's not all-or-nothing?"
Rotterdam pieces are done. Sent photos to the curator. She responded within an hour saying they were "exactly what we discussed." Submitted everything to Pieter for documentation. Moving on.

Opening night for a small group show at a friend's project space. Nothing prestigious, just artists showing work they believe in to thirty people drinking cheap wine. I almost didn't go because I had grant applications to finish, but Sanne insisted.

The work was uneven — some pieces felt student-level, honestly — but there was this energy in the room that I haven't felt at my own openings in years. People were actually talking about the art, arguing about it, getting close to the pieces instead of networking in corners. One painter had hung these massive chaotic canvases that broke every rule about composition and I stood in front of them for ten minutes feeling something I couldn't name.

On the train home I thought about my Rotterdam pieces hanging in a pristine white gallery where people will nod approvingly and then talk about real estate. That's success. That's what I wanted. I got everything right.

I keep telling myself that's what I wanted.

The Rotterdam curator emailed asking for an artist statement. I've written hundreds of these. I could do it in my sleep. But I've been staring at the blank document for two hours because every phrase I type sounds like someone else wrote it.

"My practice explores the intersection of..." No it doesn't. My practice explores whatever will get me into the next show.

I called my mother tonight to tell her about Rotterdam and she cried a little and said she always knew I'd make it. I thanked her and hung up and then sat in my studio surrounded by my real work — the hidden canvases, the ones no one will ever see — and felt like I was standing in someone else's life.

I don't know how to want different things. But I'm starting to think I already do.

Nudge: "What would it look like to let one of those hidden pieces out?"

Response: One step at a time. Rotterdam first.

Vikram got promoted to Staff Engineer today. Everyone's congratulating him on Slack, and yes, fine, he deserved it. But let's be honest—he's been here six months longer than me and his scope was smaller until Q2. I'm not bitter. I'm just aware of where I stand. My manager keeps telling me my time will come, but I've heard that before at my last company. You have to make your time come.

Called Appa tonight. He asked about work, and I told him everything was progressing well. No point worrying him with the details. He mentioned the temple renovation fund and I sent extra this month. These things matter. You take care of your family, you do what's expected—that's not a burden, that's just how it should be.

Nudge: "What would 'making your time come' actually look like?"

Response: Visibility. I need to be leading cross-functional initiatives, not just delivering my own sprint work. I've already started attending product roadmap meetings even when I'm not required. When the reorg happens in Q4, I want my name to come up naturally for the platform team lead role.
Priya asked when I'm taking her to meet my parents. I said after Diwali, maybe. We both know I'm stalling but she didn't push.

Three nights of on-call rotations. The junior devs can't seem to handle basic incident triage without escalating to me. I don't mind being the one they depend on, but I need sleep.

Nudge: "Is there a difference between being needed and being indispensable?"

Response: Indispensable means they can't function without you. Needed just means they prefer having you around. I'd rather be indispensable, even if it costs me sleep. Nobody promotes the guy who's easily replaced.

Quarterly review feedback came in. My manager said I have strong technical skills but need to demonstrate more "executive presence." I asked what that meant specifically. She said it's about how I communicate upward—being more concise in leadership meetings, framing problems in terms of business impact rather than technical detail.

I've always believed that doing excellent work should speak for itself. That's what my father taught me—results matter, not showmanship. But apparently that's not how it works here. The people who get ahead are the ones who know how to package themselves.

Spent the evening reading articles about executive communication. Downloaded a book on stakeholder management. I don't like that I have to play this game, but I've never failed at something I committed to learning. If this is what they want, I'll figure it out.

What frustrates me is that nobody told me the rules changed. At IIT, you studied hard, you got the marks, you got the placement. Clear metrics. Here it's all subjective—"presence," "influence," "visibility." How do you measure that? How do you know if you're winning?

Nudge: "What would it mean if excellent work alone was never enough?"

Response: Then everything I was taught about how success works would be wrong. My father worked for the same company for thirty years and retired as a senior manager—never played politics, just delivered results. But maybe that's why he stayed senior manager. Maybe there was a ceiling I didn't see. I don't want that ceiling.

Presented the Q3 roadmap to the leadership team today. Kept it to seven slides, used business metrics instead of technical specs. VP nodded twice. Small win.

Nudge: "How did it feel to communicate that way?"

Priya's friends are getting engaged left and right. She showed me photos from another mehendi last night and I could feel her watching for my reaction. I said the bride looked happy. That's not the response she wanted.

It's not that I don't want to marry her. I do. But there's an order to these things. My parents expect me to be settled—flat, savings, stable position—before I take that step. Amma specifically said not to repeat what her brother did, rushing into marriage without foundation and then struggling for years. I'm building the foundation. Priya should understand that this is for us, not against us.

My cousin Karthik called to announce he's getting married. He's two years younger than me, works at an MNC doing standard IT support, lives in a rented flat in Coimbatore. And yet he's the one Amma spent twenty minutes congratulating on the phone tonight. "Such good news, beta! When will we hear from you?"

I'm the one who sends money home. I'm the one who got into IIT, who moved to Bangalore for better opportunities, who's actually building something. Karthik posts cricket memes and calls it a day. But because he's checking the marriage box, suddenly he's the responsible one?

I'm not jealous of Karthik. His ceiling is that MNC job until retirement. I want more. But I can't help feeling like the family scoreboard doesn't track the things I'm actually achieving. It's all marriage, children, temple visits. The professional stuff—they're proud, sure, but they don't understand it. To them it's just "computer work."

What if I never make them understand? What if I keep climbing and the only thing they see is the boxes I haven't checked?

Nudge: "Whose approval matters more to you right now?"

The platform team lead role opened up officially. My manager submitted my name. I've been preparing all week—reviewing my contributions, documenting the initiatives I've led, practicing how to frame my scope expansion.

Interview is next Thursday. Five panelists including the CTO. I've never presented to him before. My manager said he values people who "think like owners"—whatever that means. I'm guessing it means speaking about company strategy as if you have personal stake in it. I can do that.

Called Priya to tell her. She was supportive but also asked if this meant more late nights. I said probably yes, at least initially. She went quiet. I told her once I get this role, things will stabilize. She said I said that about the senior engineer promotion too. She's not wrong, but what does she expect? This is how you build something. You sacrifice now for position later.

Appa would understand. He always said the early years are for investment, the later years for enjoying the returns. I'm still investing.

Interview went well, I think. CTO asked how I'd handle a direct report who consistently missed deadlines. I said I'd have a direct conversation about expectations and consequences. He seemed to like that.

Nudge: "What's making you unsure about how it went?"

Response: It's not uncertainty, it's just being realistic. You never know what other candidates said or what factors matter to them. I did my part. Now I wait.

Got the role. Platform Team Lead. Officially starts August 15th. Vikram sent me a congratulatory message that was probably through gritted teeth. Doesn't matter. I'm ahead now.

Manager said the CTO specifically mentioned my clarity about accountability. Direct conversations about expectations and consequences—turns out that's exactly his management philosophy. Lucky guess, or maybe I just think the way leaders do. Either way, I'm in.

Nudge: "What does being 'ahead' give you?"

Called home to share the news. Appa congratulated me, asked what the new title means in terms of responsibilities. Explained about headcount, budget ownership, technical direction. He said it sounds like a lot of work. Then asked if this means I'll be too busy to come for Pongal.

I said of course I'll come for Pongal. I always come for Pongal.

Amma asked about Priya. I said we're doing well. She said Karthik's wedding is in November and the family will be asking questions. I said I know. She said maybe Priya could come for the wedding, meet everyone. I said I'd think about it.

After the call, I sat with this feeling I couldn't name. I achieved exactly what I set out to achieve six months ago. Platform Team Lead before 32. Higher salary, direct reports, visibility to the C-suite. But the call home felt like two different conversations—me trying to share a professional milestone, them trying to pull me back to the family timeline.

Maybe both things can be true. Maybe I can lead the platform team and also bring Priya to Karthik's wedding and also send money home and also make Pongal. But it feels like I'm running two races on parallel tracks, and I'm not sure if either one knows the other exists.

Nudge: "What would it look like if both tracks knew about each other?"

Response: I'd have to tell my parents what the promotion actually cost—the late nights, the stress, why I haven't proposed yet. And I'd have to tell Priya that family expectations aren't just background noise to me, they're load-bearing. I don't know if anyone wants that level of honesty. Maybe it's easier to keep the tracks separate.

People act like delivery drivers don't know anything. I managed forty people at that factory. I know how to read a room, how to negotiate, how to plan logistics. Just because I'm on a scooter now doesn't mean I forgot everything.

Nudge: "When did someone last treat you like you didn't know anything?"

Decent week overall. Made about NT$18,000, which is slightly above average. The weather has been cooperating - no heavy rain, which means more orders and fewer accidents. I've been mapping out which restaurants have the fastest pickup times during dinner rush. Found that the smaller family-run places actually have better turnaround than the chain restaurants because they're not juggling ten delivery apps at once.

My wife mentioned that her store might cut hours in January. She said it casually, like it was nothing, but I saw her checking our savings account balance on her phone afterward. We have enough for three months, maybe four if we're careful. I've been taking the late-night shifts that nobody wants - the ones that end at 2 AM. The surge pricing is better then, and there's less competition from the younger drivers.

The app keeps pushing this "Diamond Driver" program where you get priority orders if you maintain high ratings. I'm at 4.92 right now. Would need to stay above 4.9 for two months straight. It's sort of worth it, I guess. Priority access means more consistent income.

Nudge: "What's the part that makes it only 'sort of' worth it?"

Response: Just takes a lot of energy to keep the smile on, you know? Anyway, it's fine.

My brother called tonight. He lives in Kaohsiung, works at a bank. He asked how the "side job" was going. I told him it's not a side job, it's my job. He made that sound, that little "ah" that means he doesn't believe me but won't argue.

He always does this. Acts concerned but really he just wants to feel better about his own choices. He stayed at the same bank for twenty years, never took a single risk. And now he acts like I failed at something when really the factory failed. It wasn't my decision to close it. I didn't make the products nobody wanted to buy anymore.

My son was home for the weekend and heard part of the call. He didn't say anything but I could tell he was uncomfortable. Later I heard him tell my wife that maybe I should "explore other options." What options? I'm 48. The HR screening software throws out resumes from anyone over 40 before a human even sees them. I know this because I used to be on the other side of that process.

I'm not going to apologize for doing what needs to be done to keep this family running. The gig economy isn't some failure - it's adaptation. That's what I taught my team back at the factory: adapt or get left behind. I'm adapting.

Nudge: "What did it feel like hearing your son say that?"

Can't stop thinking about my old office. The chair, the window that looked out at the parking lot, the way people would knock before coming in. Nobody knocks for delivery drivers. They just grab the food and close the door.

Customer gave me one star because the restaurant forgot her drink. I explained it wasn't my fault. She said "whatever" and shut the door. My rating dropped to 4.88. Three weeks of perfect service, gone because of someone else's mistake.

Long day. Started at 11 AM, just got home at midnight. Made NT$2,800 which is okay. My back hurts from the scooter. The seat needs replacing but that's another NT$1,500 I don't want to spend right now.

Ran into Mr. Huang at a pickup. He used to work in purchasing at the factory - we'd have lunch together sometimes. He pretended not to recognize me. Put on his sunglasses even though we were inside, grabbed his order, walked away fast. I wanted to say something but what would I say? "Remember when we both had offices?" It's fine. People handle things differently. At least I'm not the one hiding.

Nudge: "Do you actually think he was hiding from you specifically?"

Christmas. Working today because the surge pricing is excellent and most drivers take the day off to be with family. My wife wasn't happy but she understood. My son went back to Taichung for a friend's party. So it would have just been the two of us anyway.

I keep thinking about hierarchy. At the factory, I knew exactly where I stood - three levels below the director, two above the line supervisors. Clear chain of command. Now there's just the app. The app doesn't care that I have fifteen years of management experience. The app sees: delivery completed, time elapsed, customer rating. Three numbers. That's all I am now.

Some of the younger drivers formed a LINE group, share tips about which areas have better orders. They invited me but I don't participate much. They talk about this work like it's freedom - no boss, make your own hours. Maybe when you're 25 with no mortgage it feels that way. For me it feels like standing on ice that might crack any second.

My wife texted to ask when I'm coming home. She attached a photo of the dinner she made - nothing fancy, just three dishes and rice. The table looked empty with only one place setting out. I told her I'd be back by 10. It's 8:30 now and I'm sitting in my scooter outside a hot pot restaurant waiting for an order, writing this instead of going inside where it's warm.

Nudge: "Why didn't you go inside where it's warm?"

Response: Because inside I'm just a delivery guy waiting for order 847. Outside I'm just a man on a scooter. At least out here nobody's looking at me deciding what I am.

Got home at 10:30. Wife had already put everything in the fridge. She didn't say anything, just handed me a plate and went to bed. The food was cold but I ate it anyway. I'm not sure what I'm actually saving all this money for.

Year-end is always busy. Everyone ordering food instead of cooking. I should be grateful for the work. I keep telling myself that.

Today a customer - young guy, maybe late twenties, nice apartment in Da'an district - tipped me NT$100. Then he said, "Uncle, you should take care of yourself. This weather is harsh." He meant well. I know he meant well. But something about being called "Uncle" by someone half my age, living in an apartment that costs more monthly than I made in three months at the factory, offering me NT$100 like it would make a difference... I said thank you. What else could I say?

I remember when I was his age. I had just been promoted to assistant manager. I thought I would keep climbing. Director by 45, maybe VP by 50 if I played my cards right. I had a plan. The plan had steps. Now the only steps I think about are the ones between the elevator and the apartment door, hoping there's not too many flights to climb with hot soup that's getting cold.

My son called to say he got a part-time job at his university. Research assistant for one of his professors. He sounded proud. I told him that's great, focus on your studies, don't worry about money. But of course he should worry about money. Everyone should. He's watching what happened to me and thinking: I won't let that happen. And I don't know if that makes me proud or ashamed. Probably both.

Nudge: "What would make you lean more toward proud than ashamed?"

New year, new goals. Hit Diamond Driver status finally - 4.91 rating maintained for two months. Priority orders start Monday. Wife's hours weren't cut after all.

Nudge: "How did it feel when you hit Diamond?"

Response: Good, I guess. It's just a status. Means more consistent orders.

Sat in the parking garage after class for probably twenty minutes before I could make myself drive home. Stats exam went okay I think, but my brain is just done. Got a text from my sister right after saying Mom had a rough morning and kept asking where Dad was. He's been gone eleven years. Lisa sounded wrecked on the phone. I told her I'd come up this weekend to give her a break.

Now I'm staring at a stack of reading for my practicum seminar and all I can think about is whether I should have moved back to Duluth instead of doing this degree. Some days this all feels like a selfish detour when the people who need me are somewhere else.

Nudge: "What makes it feel selfish to you?"

Response: Because Lisa's up there handling everything alone while I'm down here highlighting textbooks. She didn't sign up for that. We were supposed to figure out Mom's care together and instead I enrolled in a program. I keep telling myself this degree will help me help more people long-term but honestly? Right now it just means I'm not there when my mother doesn't recognize her own daughters.

Drove up to Duluth Friday night after my last class. Lisa looked thinner than the last time I saw her, which was only three weeks ago. She's not eating enough. I spent Saturday doing Mom's laundry and sitting with her while Lisa went to get her hair done, which she hasn't done in months.

Mom was calm most of the day. She called me by my name twice, which felt like a gift. We looked through old photos and she remembered the cabin in Brainerd. Small things, but they matter. I made a big pot of chicken wild rice soup and left enough in Lisa's freezer for a week. It's the least I can do when I can't be there every day.

My cohort advisor emailed about missing a group meeting last Friday. I was in Duluth taking care of my mother. Some things are more important than a Zoom call. I'll make it up but I'm not going to apologize for being there.

Nudge: "What's it like juggling those two worlds?"

Response: It's fine, I manage. People do harder things. The advisor is understanding, I'm probably overreacting because I'm tired from the drive.

Christmas Eve and I'm wrapping presents at the kitchen table while Tyler's on the couch watching something on his phone. He's been quiet lately. I asked him if he was okay and he said yeah, just stressed about early decision. He applied to UW-Madison and won't hear back until February.

I keep wanting to fix it for him. Check his portal, email admissions, do something. But he's seventeen and he needs to learn to sit with uncertainty. That's the hardest part of caring about someone, I think. Knowing when to step back. I'm not very good at it. I'd rather stay up all night worrying on someone's behalf than let them worry alone. I don't know if that's love or just a habit I can't break.

Weird thing happened today. I was reading for my human development course, this chapter about attachment theory and caregiving patterns, and I just started crying at the kitchen table. Tyler walked in and asked what was wrong and I said nothing, just allergies, which is such a mom thing to say and we both knew it was a lie.

The chapter was about how some people develop their identity almost entirely around being needed by others. How it starts in childhood, usually with a parent who needed caretaking themselves, and it becomes this pattern where you feel most yourself when you're giving to someone else. And I'm sitting there with a highlighter in my hand thinking, this is literally my biography.

I called Lisa after Tyler went to bed. Told her about the chapter and she laughed and said yeah, no kidding, you've been like that since you were ten. She reminded me how I used to make lunches for both of us when Mom was going through her rough patches. I was in fourth grade. I don't remember it as a burden though. I remember feeling useful. Important, even.

I guess what's getting to me is wondering whether I chose social work because I genuinely want to help people or because I literally don't know how to exist without someone to take care of. That's a scary thought to put on paper. But my practicum supervisor keeps saying self-awareness is the first tool, so here it is. I'm aware. Now what.

Nudge: "Does it have to be one or the other?"

Response: Maybe not. Lisa said something similar, that both things can be true. That I can genuinely care about this work and also recognize the pattern. I just don't want to end up burning out because I never learned how to sit still without a to-do list for someone else. I watched that happen with Mom before she got sick. She ran herself ragged for everyone and then there was nobody left to run for her.

After that breakdown earlier I decided to actually be productive. Finished the attachment theory chapter and three of the discussion questions. Emailed my practicum site to confirm January hours. Called Mom's memory care coordinator to check in on the transition plan for when she needs more support, which might be sooner than we thought based on what Lisa's been describing.

The coordinator said they have a spot opening up in the assisted living wing in February. Lisa and I need to talk about it. It would take the daily pressure off Lisa but Mom would hate it. She still has enough awareness to know she's being moved, and I think that would devastate her. But Lisa can't keep doing this alone and I can't move back to Duluth right now, not with one semester left.

Made a list of things to handle this week: Tyler's FAFSA, my spring course registration, groceries for New Year's, and calling the insurance company about Mom's coverage changes. Also need to pick up Lisa's Christmas gift, which I completely forgot to order. She wanted that weighted blanket. I'll find one at Target tomorrow.

I noticed I feel calmer after making the list. That's the pattern again, isn't it. Anxiety happens and I make a list of ways to help other people and suddenly I can breathe. At least I can see it now even if I can't stop it.

Lisa just told me she's thinking about taking a leave from her job to handle Mom full-time. She can't afford that. I should be the one doing this but I'm stuck here finishing a degree that doesn't pay anyone's bills yet.

Had a long phone call with Lisa last night. We talked for almost two hours, which we haven't done in months because usually our calls are logistics. Where's the insurance card, did you call the pharmacy, can you come up this weekend. But this time we actually talked.

She told me she doesn't resent me for going back to school. I didn't realize how much I needed to hear that until she said it and I couldn't talk for a minute. She said she's proud of me, which made me feel about six years old in the best way. Then she said she's also exhausted and scared and doesn't know how long she can keep doing the day-to-day with Mom by herself.

We made a plan. I'll come up every other weekend starting this month, and we're going to look into the assisted living option together. Not making any decisions yet, just touring the place and talking to Mom's doctor. Lisa also said she wants me to stop apologizing every time we talk. She said, "You're doing what you need to do. Stop acting like you're abandoning us." I wrote that on a sticky note and put it on my laptop.

Tyler overheard part of the conversation and came in after to ask if Grandma was okay. I told him the truth, that she's not getting better but we're figuring it out as a family. He said he'd come with me to Duluth next time. He didn't have to offer that. Moments like that make me think I must have done something right with him.

Nudge: "What did it feel like to hear Lisa say she's proud of you?"

A classmate in my cohort made a comment during seminar about how people who go into social work are usually "working out their own stuff." She said it like it was a joke but half the room went quiet. I wanted to say something but I just sat there. She's twenty-six and has never had to take care of anyone but herself.

That's not fair, I don't know her story. But it bothered me more than it should have. Maybe because I know she's not wrong, at least in my case. But there's a difference between understanding your own patterns and having some kid with no life experience reduce your entire motivation to a punchline. I'm here because I've lived this. That counts for something.

Nudge: "You said she's not wrong. What part felt true?"

Response: The part about working out your own stuff, obviously. But everyone does that. Every doctor who lost a parent to cancer, every teacher who had a bad one growing up. That's not a weakness, that's fuel. I just didn't appreciate the delivery.

Drove up to Duluth Saturday morning. Tyler came with me. He sat with Mom for an hour while Lisa and I toured the assisted living wing. It's clean, the staff seemed attentive, there's a garden area for warmer months. Mom would have a private room.

Lisa and I agreed we'll bring it up with Mom's doctor at the next appointment. Neither of us said it out loud but we both know this is probably happening in the next few months. On the drive home Tyler was quiet. Then he said, "She remembered my name today." I just reached over and squeezed his hand.

Made it home by eight. Did laundry, packed Tyler's lunch for Monday, prepped my notes for practicum. Ordinary things that felt good to do.
Spring semester started. First class back and I actually felt ready. Not fixed, not finished figuring anything out. Just present. Lisa texted me good luck this morning with a heart emoji. I saved it.

I don't care what Priya thinks, I handled that IV alarm correctly. The patient was stable and I had already checked the line twice before she came over to "help." I've been on this unit for fourteen months now, I know the protocols.

Nudge: "What made her stepping in feel different from just helping out?"

Response: Because it was in front of the resident. Made me look like I didn't know what I was doing.

Still thinking about the Priya thing from earlier. I know I sounded annoyed in my last entry but there's more to it. She's been a nurse for nine years and sometimes she does this thing where she swoops in and takes over, and the doctors start looking at her for answers instead of me. I worked hard to get assigned to the step-down unit. Most new grads end up on med-surg for at least two years before they get a chance at cardiac, and I pushed for this placement because I want to be in the ICU by the time I'm twenty-five.

But here's what bugs me. When Mr. Okonkwo's wife was crying in the hallway after the cardiologist explained his prognosis, Priya was charting. I was the one who sat with Mrs. Okonkwo for twenty minutes, got her water, called her daughter. Nobody saw that. Nobody writes that in your evaluation. The doctors remember who looked competent with the IV pump, not who held someone's hand. And I did both today, but only one of those things is going to show up when they're deciding who moves to the ICU.

I'm not saying Priya's a bad nurse. She's great, actually. She taught me half of what I know about reading telemetry strips. I just wish the people making decisions about my career could see the full picture instead of whatever three-second snapshot they happened to catch.

Nudge: "You mentioned two things you did today — the clinical work and sitting with Mrs. Okonkwo. If you had to pick one to be known for, which would it be?"

Response: Both. That's the whole point. I don't want to pick. But if I'm being honest, the Mrs. Okonkwo thing is the reason I became a nurse in the first place. The ICU stuff, the certifications — that's how you get taken seriously so you can keep doing the other part. At least that's what I tell myself.
Three twelves in a row. Came home, microwaved leftover pasta, opened the CCRN study guide, and fell asleep on page four. Mom called but I let it go to voicemail. I'll call her back tomorrow.

Funny day. Got pulled into a rapid response on 4 North because they were short-staffed and the charge nurse remembered me from orientation. Patient was this 80-year-old guy, former high school football coach, and even while we were stabilizing him he was trying to crack jokes about his offensive line being tougher than his heart. We got him transferred to the ICU and the attending told me I did good work. Wrote it down in my phone immediately so I don't forget the exact words — "good clinical instinct." That's going in my ICU application.

Then I got back to my floor and found out my patient in room 412, Mrs. Patel, had been asking for me the whole time I was gone. She doesn't like the other nurses because she says they talk too fast and don't explain things. I felt bad. I sat with her for an extra ten minutes at the end of my shift even though I was already in overtime. My charge nurse gave me a look but Mrs. Patel smiled and said I remind her of her granddaughter, so whatever.

Nudge: "You stayed late for Mrs. Patel even after the charge nurse's look. What was going through your head in that moment?"

Response: It wasn't a big deal. Ten minutes. She needed someone to listen.

I'm so mad at myself. Mom needed me to drive her to her follow-up appointment today and I completely forgot because I swapped shifts to cover for Dana, who's going through a divorce and needed someone to take her Saturday. I've been telling everyone yes all week — yes I'll cover your shift, yes I'll precept the new grad, yes I'll sit on the unit council — and then I forgot the one thing that actually mattered.

My brother Tyler had to take the bus from school and then drive her in my car, and he's only had his license for three months. He was fine about it but Mom sounded so tired on the phone when she told me. Not angry, just tired. That's worse. If she yelled at me I could at least argue back but the quiet disappointment just sits in my chest like a rock.

The thing is, I covered for Dana because she was falling apart in the break room and I couldn't just walk away from that. And the new grad, Jess, she literally doesn't know how to titrate a drip yet and if I don't teach her someone's going to get hurt. And the unit council looks good on my resume for the ICU transfer. Every single thing I said yes to had a reason. But all those reasons don't change the fact that my mom sat in a waiting room chair that hurts her hip because I wasn't there.

I need to figure out how to stop doing this. I keep spreading myself across everyone else's emergencies and then there's nothing left for the people who were there first.

Nudge: "You listed the reasons for every yes. Is there one you would take back?"

Response: The unit council. That one was for me, not for anyone else. The rest I'd do again because people needed help. But the council meeting — I could have said next quarter. I just didn't want to lose the spot.

Called Mom after my shift ended. She said the appointment went fine, the surgeon is happy with her range of motion, and Tyler did a good job driving. She told me not to worry about it.

I ordered grocery delivery to her house for the week and signed up for a meal prep service for Tyler so he doesn't just eat ramen every night. Set a recurring reminder in my phone for all her upcoming appointments. I won't miss another one.

Studied for three hours this morning before my shift. Went through the hemodynamic monitoring chapter and took a practice quiz — scored 82%, which is passing but not where I want to be. My target is 90% on every practice test before I schedule the real exam. I made flashcards for the concepts I missed and I'm going to review them on my breaks this week.

At work, had a good shift. Got assigned to a fresh post-cath patient and managed the sheath pull site without any complications. Documented everything meticulously because I know the ICU charge nurses pull charts when they're reviewing internal transfer candidates. My manager Denise mentioned that there might be an opening on the ICU in March. She didn't promise anything but she said my name had come up.

After work, drove to Mom's even though it wasn't Sunday. Tyler had a rough day at school — some issue with a group project where his partners aren't pulling their weight. I helped him outline a plan for talking to his teacher about it. He's so much like me, takes everything on himself instead of asking for help. I told him it's okay to flag when something isn't working. The irony of giving that advice was not lost on me.

Nudge: "What irony were you thinking of?"

Had coffee with Priya before shift today. She told me she's leaving — transferring to a hospital in Phoenix because her husband got a job out there. I was surprised by how much it bothered me. We're not close exactly, but she's the person I learned the most from on this unit. She said she thinks I'm ready for the ICU and that she told Denise the same thing.

I should feel good about that. And part of me does. But there's this other part that's uneasy. If Priya leaves, I'm suddenly one of the more experienced nurses on the floor, and we have two new grads starting next month. That means more precepting, more questions, more of me being the person everyone leans on. I already can't say no. What happens when there's literally no one else to ask? I keep thinking about last week, forgetting Mom's appointment because I was stretched too thin. If the workload doubles, something is going to break. I just don't know if it'll be my schedule or me.

Nudge: "You said something will break. What would protecting yourself look like?"

Response: I honestly don't know. Saying no, I guess. But saying no to a new grad who doesn't know how to read a rhythm strip feels like putting a patient at risk. And saying no to my family isn't an option. So I don't really know what's left to cut.
CCRN practice test today, scored 88%. Getting closer. Tyler's coming over for dinner tonight, I'm making Mom's enchilada recipe.

Tyler told me over dinner that he's thinking about nursing school. He said he wants to be like me. I had to go to the kitchen for a minute because I didn't want him to see me tear up. It's the best thing anyone has said to me in months and also the thing that scares me the most.

Because what does he see? He sees me working hard, yes. He sees me helping people. But does he also see the dark circles and the missed appointments and the fact that I eat most of my meals standing up in a break room? I want him to have what I have — this feeling of doing something that matters — but I want him to do it without grinding himself down the way I do. I told him I thought it was a great idea and that I'd help him look at programs. And I meant it. I just hope he's better at the balance part than I am.

Nudge: "What would you want him to do differently than you?"

Response: Ask for help before he's drowning. Take a day off without feeling guilty. Remember that being good at your job and being there for your people shouldn't be an either-or thing. Basically everything I know I should do but don't.

The Hamburg hub finally hit ninety-six percent on-time and I didn't even get a mention in the quarterly call. Kramer presented the numbers like they were his. Sat there chewing my pen while the board nodded along.

Nudge: "What stopped you from speaking up during the call?"

Response: Politics. Kramer reports to the CEO directly. Picking a fight on camera would only hurt me.

Still stewing over the call. Lotte opened a bottle of that 2019 Brunello we'd been saving for something and I couldn't even enjoy it properly. Kept replaying the moment where Kramer said "the regional team" instead of my name. The regional team. Like the numbers came out of nowhere.

It's not just vanity, though people would say it is. When you run three sites and you restructure the nightshift rotation yourself and you push back on corporate for months to get the new sorting line funded, having your name attached to the outcome matters. It's how you move up. It's how people know who to call when the next VP seat opens.

Nudge: "What happens if the VP seat opens and they don't think of you?"

Response: Then six years of building this region into the best-performing in the company would have been a waste. And I'd have to seriously think about whether I stay or find somewhere that notices.

Two of my warehouse leads called in sick on the same day and I spent six hours on the floor sorting parcels like it's 2003. My back is wrecked. Cancelled dinner reservations at that new place in Katendrecht because I couldn't face sitting upright for two hours.

Had the one-on-one with Kramer today. Brought up the quarterly call, kept it measured. He said he appreciated my "team-first attitude" and that everyone knows who does the real work. Classic deflection. He even patted me on the shoulder on the way out, which I found condescending but smiled through anyway.

Lotte says I should document everything from now on. She's probably right. She also said I need to stop letting work ruin my evenings, because last weekend I spent the entire Saturday sailing trip checking emails on my phone instead of actually being out there. She's definitely right about that one. I barely noticed the water.

Nudge: "You said you barely noticed the water. How often does that happen — being somewhere you chose to be but not really being there?"

Response: More than I'd like. But that's the deal when you run things, isn't it.

Got the green light to hire two senior coordinators for the Antwerp hub. My budget, my picks, no committee. Finally something that feels like actual authority instead of managing upwards all day. Already have a shortlist.

Hosted twelve people for dinner last night. Lotte paired every course — she did the lamb with a Châteauneuf-du-Pape that made van der Berg's wife close her eyes when she tasted it. That's the kind of evening I live for. Good food, the right people, candles on the canal, Lotte in her element. Van der Berg even asked if we'd consider doing a supper club. I said maybe, but honestly I don't want to turn it into an obligation.

The thing is, half the guests were people from the industry. Not a coincidence. You build your network over a cheese course, not in a meeting room. Van der Berg sits on the advisory board at Port of Rotterdam. His wife runs procurement at Maersk Benelux. I like these people genuinely — I do — but I also know exactly what those relationships are worth. Lotte says I can never just have fun without an angle. She's not wrong, but I don't see why it can't be both.

Nudge: "When Lotte says you always have an angle — does that bother you or does it feel like a compliment?"

Response: Depends on the day. Sometimes I think she sees through me in a way that's useful. Other times it makes me feel like I can't just enjoy something without her questioning my motives.

Long week. The Antwerp expansion hit a snag — permits delayed, which means the two coordinators I hired start in three weeks with nothing structured for them to do. I've been on calls with the municipal office, with legal, with the construction firm, trying to unstick things. None of it is technically my job. The site manager should be handling permits. But if I leave it to him we'll lose another month, and I can't afford that with the VP shortlist conversation supposedly happening in Q3.

Lotte and I were supposed to go to Bruges for the weekend. She booked a hotel with a rooftop terrace and a Michelin-star restaurant for Saturday night. I told her I'd need to work Saturday morning and she went quiet in that way she does. Not angry, just — recalibrating. She went anyway, took her friend Anneke. I stayed home and spent the day on a laptop in my bathrobe eating cheese toast. By four o'clock I realised the permit issue was probably going to take another two weeks regardless of whether I spent Saturday on it or not.

I could have been in Bruges. I could have been sitting on a rooftop with a glass of something excellent, watching the sun go down with the person I like most in the world. Instead I ate cheese toast. I don't know what to do with that information.

Nudge: "What would it take for you to have gone to Bruges instead?"

Lotte showed me photos from Bruges. The terrace, the food, the canal at sunset. She wasn't rubbing it in — she just wanted to share. I looked at the photos and felt something I can only describe as stupid. Not sad. Stupid.

Permits came through. Both coordinators start Monday and the fit-out begins next week. I walked the Antwerp floor this morning and rearranged the staging layout myself because the site manager's plan was going to create a bottleneck at dock three. He didn't love me overriding him but he'll see the throughput numbers in two weeks and come around.

Took Friday off. Actually took it off — phone in a drawer, sailed out to the Grevelingenmeer with nobody but the cats in my head for company. Grilled fish on the dock afterwards, drank a cold Riesling, watched the light change on the water for an hour. Didn't think about Kramer or permits or throughput once. Well, maybe once. Lotte would be proud. Didn't tell her about the once.

Nudge: "You mentioned the phone in the drawer like it was a big deal. Was it?"

Response: Yes. I check it probably forty times a day on weekends. Old habit from when I was on-call as a shift lead. Now it's just control.

VP shortlist leaked. Three names. I'm one of them. Kramer is not, which should make me feel vindicated but instead I just feel the weight of it. If I get this, I'm responsible for all five regions across the Benelux. Sixty direct reports through my managers. A seat at the executive table. Everything I've been manoeuvring towards for years.

But there's a clause in the offer framework that the role is based in Amsterdam and requires eighty percent in-office. Lotte's business is in Rotterdam. The canal house is in Rotterdam. The boat is twenty minutes from Rotterdam. I'd be commuting two hours a day or we'd need a second flat in Amsterdam, which means I'd be living half my life in a box near Zuidas eating takeaway alone while Lotte hosts wine tastings without me.

I keep doing the maths in my head. More status, more scope, more say in how the company runs. But also more time in transit, fewer evenings on the terrace, fewer dinners where Lotte pairs a wine so perfect it changes the conversation. I don't know how you weigh those things against each other. Every time I think I've decided, I flip. Lotte hasn't said what she thinks yet. I think she's waiting to see what I actually want before she tells me what she wants, and honestly that scares me more than the interview.

Nudge: "If Lotte told you she wanted you to take it, would that make it easier or harder?"

Response: Harder. Because then I couldn't blame the decision on protecting what we have. I'd have to admit I want it and deal with what that costs.

Finally told Andrés he can't keep showing up twenty minutes late to install pieces. I've been letting it slide for weeks because he's good with his hands and the clients like him, but this morning he rolled in while a collector was already waiting and I had to cover for him, again. So I pulled him aside after she left and laid it out: one more time and I'm finding someone else.

He looked genuinely startled, which tells me nobody's ever drawn that line with him. Good. I can't have my space looking disorganized when there's money on the table. Felt solid doing it, too. Not angry, just clear. The gallery is mine and the standard is mine to set.

Nudge: "Did the collector say anything about the wait?"

Response: She didn't mention it directly, but she kept checking her phone while I was making small talk. Ended up buying the smaller piece instead of the one she'd asked about originally. Could be coincidence, could be she lost patience. Either way, that's exactly why I can't let things slide.

Long day finishing the commission for the Restrepo family. Got the last panel mounted and stepped back to look at the whole triptych together. It works. The proportions are right, the color transitions hold. I stood there for a minute just looking at it and felt something click into place, that rare moment when what I made matches what I saw in my head.

Called my mom after. She asked about the gallery rent, whether I'd paid on time. I had, obviously. I always do. She worries, but she doesn't need to — I've had the next three months covered since January. Told her the commission check clears Friday. She sounded relieved, which is enough for now.

Nudge: "What was that click like?"

Met with a potential co-investor for the expansion into the second floor. He kept talking over me, pitching his own vision for the layout. I smiled and nodded through most of it instead of pushing back, and now I'm sitting here irritated at myself.

Walked through the Saturday market in Provenza this morning, not to buy anything but just to look at how other people display their work. The ceramicist on the corner has started using linen backdrops and better lighting — she's learning. A year ago her booth looked like a garage sale. Now people stop.

I do this thing where I watch who commands a space and who just fills it. The ceramicist commands hers now. It reminded me why I fought so hard to get the gallery out of the shared lease arrangement. When I had to negotiate every wall placement with Camilo, nothing felt fully mine. Now every corner of that space reflects a decision I made, and collectors can feel it. That matters more than people think.

Lazy Sunday, which almost never happens. Woke up late, made arepas with the leftover hogao from Friday. Sat on the balcony eating and watching the street below. The neighbor's kid was kicking a ball against the wall again, steady thunk-thunk-thunk rhythm that's almost calming when you're not trying to concentrate.
