Started going through my supply invoices because even on a day off I can't leave the numbers alone. Paints are up twelve percent from last year which is absurd, and I'm running through the cadmium yellow faster than usual because of the Restrepo palette. Need to either find a new supplier or adjust my pricing. Probably both. I've been meaning to rework the pricing sheet anyway — the last revision was August and costs have shifted.

Nothing urgent. The accounts are fine. I just like knowing exactly where the money sits, what's coming in, what's going out. My mother would say I got that from my father, who used to keep a paper ledger for his tire shop even after they had a computer. Different scale, same instinct.

Spent the afternoon finalizing the March exhibition lineup and it felt like orchestrating something, which is the part of this work I love most. Three emerging artists, each one I handpicked. Two of them came to me through referrals from collectors I've built relationships with over years. The third found me through Instagram, sent a portfolio that was raw but had something. I'm taking a risk on her, but a calculated one — I've already soft-committed two buyers who tend to go for that aesthetic.

The thing is, when I curate a show, I'm not just hanging art. I'm deciding whose career gets a platform and whose doesn't. That responsibility sits well with me. I spent years being the one hoping someone would give me a shot. Now I'm on the other side. I've earned that.

Had a good call with Daniela, my accountant, beforehand. March projections are strong, the reserve fund is untouched, insurance renewal is handled. When the financial side is solid, I can actually think about the art. When it's shaky, everything else feels shaky too. I learned that the hard way during the first year after the split with Camilo, when I was burning through savings and couldn't focus on a single canvas.

Nudge: "What was the something in her portfolio?"

Response: Honestly, she reminded me of myself ten years ago. Same raw energy, same refusal to make the work palatable for easy sales. I almost didn't reach out because I don't usually go for sentimental reasons. But then I looked at her pieces again and they're genuinely good. I just didn't want to admit that the recognition was part of why I noticed her.

The co-investor sent a revised proposal with his name listed as "co-director" of the new floor. I didn't agree to that. I wrote back saying we need to clarify terms before anything moves forward, but my hands were shaking when I hit send. Not from fear — from the idea that someone thinks they can just slide into a title I haven't offered.

Nudge: "What were the hands shaking from, then?"

Response: Just adrenaline. It's fine. The email's sent, the boundary is drawn. Moving on.

March show opens Friday. All three artists delivered on time. Walked through the hung pieces this evening and everything is exactly where I placed it. Good feeling, knowing the room is set and the invitations went out to the right people.

Opening night went better than I planned. Sold four pieces in the first two hours, including one from the new artist — the one I took a chance on. A collector from Bogotá asked for my card and said he'd been hearing my name. That sentence alone made the whole month worth it. This is what building a reputation looks like, one show at a time, one handshake at a time.

Called my mom after closing up. She asked how much I made and I told her, which I don't usually do, but I wanted her to hear it. Wanted her to know the gallery isn't some reckless bet anymore. She said "qué bueno, mija" in that tone that means she's genuinely proud but won't say it bigger than that. That's enough. The numbers are good, the reserve is intact, and people are starting to know the name Villalobos in this city's art scene.

Someone on an art forum called my curation "commercially safe." I almost replied but closed the tab instead. My gallery, my choices, my track record. If making a living from art while keeping my lights on and my artists paid is "safe," fine. I'll take it.

Nudge: "What were you going to type?"

Response: Something about how I left a stable partnership to build this alone and nobody who's done that gets to be called safe. But then I realized I'd be arguing with a stranger who's never mounted a show or signed a lease. Not worth it. I know what I've built.

The co-investor agreed to drop the co-director title but now wants approval rights on artist selection for the second floor. Told him I'd think about it. I won't give that up, but I also can't fund the expansion alone and the bank terms are worse. Feels like I'm slowly trading control for capital and I'm not sure where the line is.

Nudge: "Where would the line be?"

Response: I don't know yet. That's the whole problem. I'll figure it out before the next meeting.

My sister Huda called again about the retirement fund thing. She has this way of bringing it up like she is just asking a casual question but we both know where it is going. "Just curious, do you have anything saved for when you cannot drive anymore?" She knows the answer. She has known for years.

What she does not understand is that I did the office thing for fifteen years and it nearly killed something in me. I am not going back to counting someone else's hours so I can sit in a flat at seventy with money and nothing to show for actually living. Maybe I am wrong. But right now the idea of giving up my mornings at Abu Khalil's cafe to stack dirham in an account I might never touch makes me feel worse than the uncertainty does.

Nudge: "What does the uncertainty actually feel like?"

Response: It feels like Tuesday. Anyway, Huda worries enough for both of us.

Good week for rides. Had a couple of airport pickups that paid well and a half-day tour with a German family visiting Jerash. They wanted someone who could explain things properly, not just point at columns. Felt good using that part of my brain again. Have not done a proper tour in a while and I forgot how much I like it.

Spent the extra money on a proper dinner at Sufra with Tariq and Walid. Lamb mansaf, the real kind. We sat there for three hours talking about nothing important. Drove home with the windows down. Warm night still, even in October.

Nudge: "What made those three hours worth more than saving it?"
Long day, five rides back to back because I said yes to everything on the app. Knees ache and my shoulders are stiff. Going to bed early for once.

Cannot sleep. Lying here thinking about that German family from last week, how the father kept asking me questions about Umayyad architecture like I was a professor. I knew every answer. Fifteen years driving tourists around will do that. Strange how that one afternoon made the whole week feel worthwhile.

Nudge: "Is it the knowing, or someone caring that you know?"

Response: Both, honestly. But if nobody asks, the knowing just sits there.

Woke up still tired from yesterday's marathon. App is showing surge pricing downtown but my back says no. Skipped it. Felt relieved for ten minutes then started calculating what I am losing.

Nudge: "Which number matters more — what you lost or what your back saved you?"

Response: Ask me again when rent is due.
Went out for coffee instead of driving. Sat at Abu Khalil's and watched the street for an hour. Sometimes that is the whole day and it has to be enough.

Third time writing today which is unusual for me. Tariq stopped by the cafe this afternoon and mentioned his son got him set up on one of those delivery apps, the bicycle ones. He is sixty-two and cycling around Amman in this heat delivering shawarma. He laughed about it but I could see he was embarrassed.

That could be me in a few years if the driving work dries up. Or it could not. Hard to know. Tariq still ordered the expensive coffee though, the Turkish one with cardamom they do properly. We split a knafeh. He said the delivery money is not bad and he picks his own hours. Same thing I always say about driving. Maybe we are both just telling ourselves the right story.

Nudge: "What would the wrong story sound like?"

Response: That we gave up something real for comfort that does not last. That choosing the nice afternoon over the stable job was not brave, it was just easier. I do not fully believe that. But I hear it in Huda's voice sometimes.

Had a fare today, a businessman from Riyadh who spent the whole ride talking on his phone about quarterly targets and staff problems. Tipped well but looked miserable. Suit that cost more than my rent and he did not once look out the window at the citadel lit up at night.

People assume I ended up here, like gig work is where you land when everything else falls through. Maybe for some. For me it was a door I walked through on purpose. I know what a Monday morning in an office smells like. I know what it costs. I chose this, and today, watching that man bark into his phone while Amman glowed outside his window, I felt sure about it.

Nudge: "Do you always feel this sure, or just on nights like this?"

Response: Not always. The sure feeling comes and goes. But tonight it is here so I am writing it down.

Rent went up. Landlord sent a message this morning, very polite about it, but two hundred dinars more starting January. I sat with the phone in my hand for a while doing the math. It is tight. It was already tight but now it is the kind of tight where one bad week matters.

Part of me wants to call Huda and tell her she was right, just get it over with. But right does not mean I know what to do differently. I cannot go back to an office. I do not mean I refuse, I mean something in me genuinely cannot. The last time I tried I lasted four months and stopped sleeping. So what is the alternative. Pick up more rides, skip the cafe, eat at home every night. Become the version of me that survives but does not particularly want to.

Later in the evening now. Calmed down a bit. Called Walid and he said his cousin has a spare room in Jabal Amman, smaller place but cheaper, and the neighborhood is actually nicer for walking. Might look at it this weekend. Moving is not ideal at fifty-eight but it is not the end of the world either.

I keep thinking about what I wrote earlier about not being able to go back to office work. That is true but it is also a wall I have built. The real question is whether the wall is protecting something important or just keeping me stuck. I think it is protecting something. The mornings where I sit with coffee and watch the light hit the old stones. The afternoons where I take the long route home because the view from the fourth circle is good at sunset. The dinners that go three hours because nobody has anywhere to be.

These are small things. Nobody would list them on a CV or count them as assets. But they are the thread running through every good day I have had in the past fifteen years. If I have to move to a smaller flat to keep them, that is an easy trade. If I have to drive more shifts, I can do that too. What I cannot do is give them up entirely and still recognize myself.

Nudge: "When did you first realize those small things were the thread?"

Response: Probably the week I quit the logistics job. I walked out at lunch and sat in a park eating falafel and thought, this is the first time in years I have actually tasted my food. That was fifteen years ago and I still remember the taste.

People act like delivery drivers don't know anything. I managed forty people at that factory. I know how to read a room, how to negotiate, how to plan logistics. Just because I'm on a scooter now doesn't mean I forgot everything.

Nudge: "When did someone last treat you like you didn't know anything?"

Decent week overall. Made about NT$18,000, which is slightly above average. The weather has been cooperating - no heavy rain, which means more orders and fewer accidents. I've been mapping out which restaurants have the fastest pickup times during dinner rush. Found that the smaller family-run places actually have better turnaround than the chain restaurants because they're not juggling ten delivery apps at once.

My wife mentioned that her store might cut hours in January. She said it casually, like it was nothing, but I saw her checking our savings account balance on her phone afterward. We have enough for three months, maybe four if we're careful. I've been taking the late-night shifts that nobody wants - the ones that end at 2 AM. The surge pricing is better then, and there's less competition from the younger drivers.

The app keeps pushing this "Diamond Driver" program where you get priority orders if you maintain high ratings. I'm at 4.92 right now. Would need to stay above 4.9 for two months straight. It's sort of worth it, I guess. Priority access means more consistent income.

Nudge: "What's the part that makes it only 'sort of' worth it?"

Response: Just takes a lot of energy to keep the smile on, you know? Anyway, it's fine.

My brother called tonight. He lives in Kaohsiung, works at a bank. He asked how the "side job" was going. I told him it's not a side job, it's my job. He made that sound, that little "ah" that means he doesn't believe me but won't argue.

He always does this. Acts concerned but really he just wants to feel better about his own choices. He stayed at the same bank for twenty years, never took a single risk. And now he acts like I failed at something when really the factory failed. It wasn't my decision to close it. I didn't make the products nobody wanted to buy anymore.

My son was home for the weekend and heard part of the call. He didn't say anything but I could tell he was uncomfortable. Later I heard him tell my wife that maybe I should "explore other options." What options? I'm 48. The HR screening software throws out resumes from anyone over 40 before a human even sees them. I know this because I used to be on the other side of that process.

I'm not going to apologize for doing what needs to be done to keep this family running. The gig economy isn't some failure - it's adaptation. That's what I taught my team back at the factory: adapt or get left behind. I'm adapting.

Nudge: "What did it feel like hearing your son say that?"

Can't stop thinking about my old office. The chair, the window that looked out at the parking lot, the way people would knock before coming in. Nobody knocks for delivery drivers. They just grab the food and close the door.

Customer gave me one star because the restaurant forgot her drink. I explained it wasn't my fault. She said "whatever" and shut the door. My rating dropped to 4.88. Three weeks of perfect service, gone because of someone else's mistake.

Long day. Started at 11 AM, just got home at midnight. Made NT$2,800 which is okay. My back hurts from the scooter. The seat needs replacing but that's another NT$1,500 I don't want to spend right now.

Ran into Mr. Huang at a pickup. He used to work in purchasing at the factory - we'd have lunch together sometimes. He pretended not to recognize me. Put on his sunglasses even though we were inside, grabbed his order, walked away fast. I wanted to say something but what would I say? "Remember when we both had offices?" It's fine. People handle things differently. At least I'm not the one hiding.

Nudge: "Do you actually think he was hiding from you specifically?"

Christmas. Working today because the surge pricing is excellent and most drivers take the day off to be with family. My wife wasn't happy but she understood. My son went back to Taichung for a friend's party. So it would have just been the two of us anyway.

I keep thinking about hierarchy. At the factory, I knew exactly where I stood - three levels below the director, two above the line supervisors. Clear chain of command. Now there's just the app. The app doesn't care that I have fifteen years of management experience. The app sees: delivery completed, time elapsed, customer rating. Three numbers. That's all I am now.

Some of the younger drivers formed a LINE group, share tips about which areas have better orders. They invited me but I don't participate much. They talk about this work like it's freedom - no boss, make your own hours. Maybe when you're 25 with no mortgage it feels that way. For me it feels like standing on ice that might crack any second.

My wife texted to ask when I'm coming home. She attached a photo of the dinner she made - nothing fancy, just three dishes and rice. The table looked empty with only one place setting out. I told her I'd be back by 10. It's 8:30 now and I'm sitting in my scooter outside a hot pot restaurant waiting for an order, writing this instead of going inside where it's warm.

Nudge: "Why didn't you go inside where it's warm?"

Response: Because inside I'm just a delivery guy waiting for order 847. Outside I'm just a man on a scooter. At least out here nobody's looking at me deciding what I am.

Got home at 10:30. Wife had already put everything in the fridge. She didn't say anything, just handed me a plate and went to bed. The food was cold but I ate it anyway. I'm not sure what I'm actually saving all this money for.

Year-end is always busy. Everyone ordering food instead of cooking. I should be grateful for the work. I keep telling myself that.

Today a customer - young guy, maybe late twenties, nice apartment in Da'an district - tipped me NT$100. Then he said, "Uncle, you should take care of yourself. This weather is harsh." He meant well. I know he meant well. But something about being called "Uncle" by someone half my age, living in an apartment that costs more monthly than I made in three months at the factory, offering me NT$100 like it would make a difference... I said thank you. What else could I say?

I remember when I was his age. I had just been promoted to assistant manager. I thought I would keep climbing. Director by 45, maybe VP by 50 if I played my cards right. I had a plan. The plan had steps. Now the only steps I think about are the ones between the elevator and the apartment door, hoping there's not too many flights to climb with hot soup that's getting cold.

My son called to say he got a part-time job at his university. Research assistant for one of his professors. He sounded proud. I told him that's great, focus on your studies, don't worry about money. But of course he should worry about money. Everyone should. He's watching what happened to me and thinking: I won't let that happen. And I don't know if that makes me proud or ashamed. Probably both.

Nudge: "What would make you lean more toward proud than ashamed?"

New year, new goals. Hit Diamond Driver status finally - 4.91 rating maintained for two months. Priority orders start Monday. Wife's hours weren't cut after all.

Nudge: "How did it feel when you hit Diamond?"

Response: Good, I guess. It's just a status. Means more consistent orders.
